# rws â€” Rust Web Server

> Source: https://github.com/bohdaq/rust-web-server
> Website: https://rws8.tech/
> Crates.io: https://crates.io/crates/rust-web-server
> Docs: https://docs.rs/rust-web-server
> License: MIT  |  MSRV: 1.75  |  Current version: 17.60.0

`rws` is an HTTP web framework, reverse proxy, and standalone server for Rust.
It supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3/QUIC over TLS with no third-party HTTP
dependencies â€” all HTTP parsing, routing, middleware, WebSocket, SSE, CORS,
rate limiting, and MIME detection are built from scratch.

Use it in three modes:
- **Static file server** â€” `cargo install rust-web-server && rws`
- **Config-driven proxy** â€” drop `rws.config.toml` with `[[route]]`/`[[upstream]]`; no code required
- **Library crate** â€” `cargo add rust-web-server`; use as a full application framework

---

## Key files

- [README.md](README.md) â€” overview, feature list, quick starts
- [DEVELOPER.md](DEVELOPER.md) â€” full building-blocks reference + 78 use-case examples
- [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) â€” architecture, request lifecycle, module index, coding conventions
- [Cargo.toml](Cargo.toml) â€” feature flags and optional dependencies
- [src/lib.rs](src/lib.rs) â€” crate root; all public modules declared here
- [src/main.rs](src/main.rs) â€” binary entry point; builds the server using the library API
- [spec/](spec/) â€” design specs for major features (proxy, SSO, model layer, etc.)

---

## Architecture

### Request lifecycle (default `http3` build)

```
main.rs
  â””â”€ build_app()              â†’ returns Box<dyn Application>
  â””â”€ Server::setup()          â†’ binds TCP listener, creates runtime
  â””â”€ tokio::join!(
       Server::run_tls(),     â†’ accepts TLS; ALPN â†’ HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1
       Server::run_quic(),    â†’ accepts QUIC â†’ HTTP/3
       Server::run_redirect() â†’ optional HTTPâ†’HTTPS redirect port
     )

Per connection (HTTP/1.1 TLS):
  Server::process() â†’ read bytes â†’ Request::parse()
                    â†’ app.execute(request, connection) â†’ Response
                    â†’ apply gzip â†’ write bytes

Per connection (HTTP/2):
  h2_handler::handle_connection() â†’ translates h2 frames â†’ app.execute() â†’ h2 frames

Per connection (HTTP/3):
  h3_handler â†’ RequestResolver::resolve_request() â†’ app.execute() â†’ h3 response
```

### Application trait

Every application variant implements `Application`:
```rust
pub trait Application: Send + Sync {
    fn execute(&self, request: &Request, connection: &ConnectionInfo) -> Result<Response, String>;
}
```

`App::execute` walks a hardcoded list of `Controller::is_matching` checks.
`AppWithState<S>::execute` uses a dynamic `Router` with registered handlers.
`WithMiddleware<A>::execute` calls each `Middleware::handle` layer in order.

### Middleware trait

```rust
pub trait Middleware: Send + Sync {
    fn handle(&self, request: &Request, connection: &ConnectionInfo, next: &dyn Application)
        -> Result<Response, String>;
}
```

Call `next.execute(request, connection)` to pass through, or return a `Response`
directly to short-circuit. Layers are applied in registration order (first `.wrap()`
is outermost). Any `Application` can be wrapped: `app.wrap(layer)`.

### Key types

```
Request         src/request/mod.rs       â€” method: String, request_uri: String,
                                           http_version: String, headers: Vec<Header>,
                                           body: Vec<u8>

Response        src/response/mod.rs      â€” status_code: i16, reason_phrase: String,
                                           headers: Vec<Header>,
                                           content_range_list: Vec<ContentRange>,
                                           stream_file: Option<String>,
                                           stream_pipe: Option<Box<dyn Read + Send>>
                                           (pipe bytes to client via chunked encoding;
                                            set by ReverseProxy for SSE / AI streams / large downloads)

Header          src/header/mod.rs        â€” name: String, value: String
                                           (constants: Header::_CONTENT_TYPE, etc.)

ConnectionInfo  src/server/mod.rs        â€” client: Address { ip, port },
                                           server: Address { ip, port },
                                           request_size: usize,
                                           sni_hostname: Option<String>

STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE src/response/mod.rs
                         â€” typed constants: .n200_ok, .n302_found,
                           .n400_bad_request, .n401_unauthorized,
                           .n403_forbidden, .n404_not_found, .n500_internal_server_error, â€¦
                           each has .status_code: &i16 and .reason_phrase: &str

Range::get_content_range(body: Vec<u8>, mime: String) â†’ ContentRange
    src/range/mod.rs  â€” standard way to set a response body

MimeType::TEXT_PLAIN, MimeType::APPLICATION_JSON, MimeType::TEXT_HTML, â€¦
    src/mime_type/mod.rs â€” MIME type constants; MimeType::detect("file.rs") â†’ &str
```

### Building a response (canonical pattern)

```rust
use crate::response::{Response, STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE};
use crate::range::Range;
use crate::mime_type::MimeType;
use crate::header::Header;

let mut r = Response::new();
r.status_code = *STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE.n200_ok.status_code;
r.reason_phrase = STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE.n200_ok.reason_phrase.to_string();
r.content_range_list = vec![Range::get_content_range(
    b"Hello".to_vec(),
    MimeType::TEXT_PLAIN.to_string(),
)];
r.headers.push(Header { name: "X-Custom".to_string(), value: "value".to_string() });
```

### JSON / text response shortcut (no `serde` feature needed)

```rust
use crate::response::{Response, STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE};

// application/json â€” body is already-serialized bytes (e.g. serde_json::to_vec(&value)?)
let r = Response::json(STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE.n200_ok, br#"{"ok":true}"#.to_vec());

// text/plain
let r = Response::text(STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE.n400_bad_request, "invalid input");
```

For a typed `Serialize` value in one call (handles serialization for you), use `Json(value).into_response()` instead (`serde` feature â€” see `src/json/`).

### 302 Redirect pattern

```rust
let mut r = Response::new();
r.status_code = *STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE.n302_found.status_code;
r.reason_phrase = STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE.n302_found.reason_phrase.to_string();
r.headers.push(Header { name: "Location".to_string(), value: "/destination".to_string() });
```

### Typed error pattern

```rust
use crate::error::{AppError, IntoResponse};

// Returns 403 Forbidden:
Ok(AppError::Forbidden.into_response())

// Returns 404 Not Found with message:
Ok(AppError::NotFound("user not found".into()).into_response())

// Full enum: BadRequest(String), Unauthorized, Forbidden,
//            NotFound(String), Conflict(String),
//            UnprocessableEntity(String), TooManyRequests, Internal(String)
```

---

## Application variants

### App (zero-config)

`App::new()` â€” wraps all built-in controllers (static files, healthz, readyz,
metrics, CORS, SSE, WebSocket, MCP). Minimal boilerplate. Suitable for static
serving or adding a few custom controllers.

### AppWithState<S> (state + routing)

```rust
let app = App::with_state(MyState { â€¦ })
    .get("/users/:id", get_user)
    .post("/users", create_user)
    .put("/users/:id", update_user)
    .delete("/users/:id", delete_user)
    .wrap(RateLimitLayer);

// Handler signature:
fn get_user(req: &Request, params: &PathParams, conn: &ConnectionInfo, state: &Arc<MyState>)
    -> Response { â€¦ }
```

`PathParams::get("id")` extracts named path segments. `*wildcard` matches trailing paths.

### AsyncAppWithState<S> (async handlers, requires `http2`)

```rust
let app = App::with_async_state(MyState { â€¦ })
    .get("/users", list_users);

async fn list_users(req: &Request, params: &PathParams, conn: &ConnectionInfo, state: &Arc<MyState>)
    -> Response { â€¦ }
```

### routes! macro (declarative table, requires `macros`)

```rust
let app = routes! {
    App::with_state(my_state),
    GET  "/users"     => list_users,
    POST "/users"     => create_user,
    GET  "/users/:id" => get_user,
};
```

### WithMiddleware<A>

```rust
// Any Application wrapped with middleware layers:
let app = App::new()
    .wrap(OtelLayer)
    .wrap(RateLimitLayer)
    .wrap(CacheLayer::new().ttl(60));
// Layers run in registration order (OtelLayer is outermost here).
```

### McpServer (Model Context Protocol)

```rust
let mcp = App::new().mcp("my-server", "1.0.0")
    .tool("greet", "Say hello", schema, |params| {
        Ok(format!("Hello, {}!", params["name"]))
    });
// Serves POST /mcp (JSON-RPC 2.0, MCP Streamable HTTP protocol).
```

---

## Feature flags

| Feature | What it enables | Key new deps |
|---|---|---|
| `http1` (no-default) | Sync thread-pool server, no tokio, no TLS | `ctrlc`, `libc` |
| `http2` | tokio, rustls TLS, HTTP/2 via `h2` crate | `tokio`, `rustls`, `h2` |
| `http3` **(default)** | HTTP/3 over QUIC via `quinn` + `h3` (implies `http2`) | `quinn`, `h3`, `h3-quinn` |
| `http-client` | HTTPS for outbound `Client` | `rustls`, `webpki-roots` |
| `serde` | `Json<T>` extractor/responder via `serde_json` | `serde`, `serde_json` |
| `auth` | `BasicAuthLayer`, `JwtLayer`, `ForwardAuthLayer`, `build_jwt`/`verify_jwt` (HS256) | `hmac`, `sha2` |
| `auth-asymmetric` | `JwtLayer::rs256`/`::es256`, `verify_jwt_rs256`/`verify_jwt_es256` (implies `auth`) | `rsa`, `p256`, `rand_core` |
| `macros` | `#[get]`, `#[derive(FromRequest)]`, `#[derive(Validate)]`, `#[derive(Config)]`, `routes!` | `rws-macros` |
| `acme` | Automatic TLS via Let's Encrypt (implies `http2`) | `rcgen`, `aws-lc-rs` |
| `tera` | Tera HTML template engine (Jinja2/Django syntax) | `tera`, `serde`, `serde_json` |
| `model-sqlite` | Async ORM backed by SQLite (implies `http2`); combinable with the other two `model-*` features (see `Backend`) | `sqlx` |
| `model-postgres` | Async ORM backed by PostgreSQL (implies `http2`); combinable with the other two `model-*` features (see `Backend`) | `sqlx` |
| `model-mysql` | Async ORM backed by MySQL (implies `http2`); combinable with the other two `model-*` features (see `Backend`) | `sqlx` |
| `crypto` | `hash_password`/`verify_password` (Argon2id), `generate_token` (CSPRNG), `signed_cookie`/`verify_signed_cookie` (HMAC-SHA256), `encrypted_cookie`/`decrypt_cookie` (AES-256-GCM) | `argon2`, `rand_core`, `hmac`, `sha2`, `aes-gcm` |
| `csrf` | `CsrfLayer` + `CsrfToken` â€” double-submit cookie CSRF protection | `rand_core` |
| `sso` | `OidcAuth` middleware, PKCE, RS256/ES256 JWT via JWKS, provider presets | `rsa`, `p256`, `sha2`, `serde`, `serde_json` |
| `sso-server` | `AuthServer` â€” OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server (HS256 token issuer); implies `sso` + `auth` | (none beyond `sso`/`auth`) |
| `sso-saml` | `SamlSp` â€” SAML 2.0 Service Provider, ACS handler, RSA-SHA256 XML signature verification; implies `sso` | (none â€” hand-rolled XML parser, not quick-xml) |
| `webhook` | `verify_webhook_signature`, `WebhookProvider` (GitHub/Shopify/Stripe HMAC verification) | `hmac`, `sha2` |
| `rewrite-regex` | `RewriteLayer::request_uri_regex_rewrite` â€” regex URI rewrite with `$1`/`${name}` capture expansion | `regex` |

Build commands:
```bash
cargo build                            # http3 (default) â€” HTTP/3 + HTTP/2 + TLS
cargo build --no-default-features --features http2   # HTTP/2 + TLS
cargo build --no-default-features --features http1   # HTTP/1.1 only, sync, no TLS
```

---

## Routing

### Dynamic Router (standalone)

```rust
use rust_web_server::router::Router;

let mut router = Router::new();
router.get("/api/users/:id", |req, params, conn| { â€¦ });
router.post("/api/users", |req, params, conn| { â€¦ });
// In Application::execute:
router.handle(request, connection)  // Returns Option<Response>
```

### Virtual-host routing

```rust
Router::new().with_host("api.example.com")
    .get("/v1/status", status_handler)
```

Matches requests whose SNI hostname (TLS) or `Host` header (plain HTTP) equals the given value.

### Path parameter syntax 

- `:name` â€” matches one path segment; `params.get("name")` â†’ `Option<&str>`
- `*name` â€” matches the rest of the path (trailing wildcard)

`App`'s own built-in controllers (static files, health/ready/metrics, forms, 404) deliberately don't
use `Router` â€” that set is small, static, and known at compile time, so a fixed if-chain is simpler
than a segment matcher there. `Router` is for user-defined routes; `AppWithState`/`AsyncAppWithState`
build on it and fall through to `App`'s controller chain for anything unmatched.

---

## Extractors (src/extract/mod.rs)

```rust
use rust_web_server::extract::{Body, BodyText, Query, RequestHeaders};

// Body â€” raw bytes, never fails
let raw: Body = Body::from_request(req)?;

// BodyText â€” UTF-8 body, 400 on invalid UTF-8
let text: BodyText = BodyText::from_request(req)?;

// Query â€” parsed query string
let q: Query = Query::from_request(req)?;
let id = q.get("id");  // Option<&String>

// RequestHeaders â€” case-insensitive header access
let h: RequestHeaders = RequestHeaders::from_request(req)?;
let ct = h.get("Content-Type");  // Option<&str>
```

Derive `FromRequest` on a struct to compose extractors:
```rust
#[derive(FromRequest)]  // requires macros feature
struct CreateUser { body: BodyText, auth: RequestHeaders }
```

---

## Validation (src/validate/mod.rs)

```rust
#[derive(FromRequest, Validate)]  // both require macros feature
struct SignupForm {
    #[validate(length(min = 3, max = 50))]
    username: BodyText,
    #[validate(email)]
    email: BodyText,
    #[validate(length(min = 8))]
    password: BodyText,
}

// In handler:
let form = Validated::<SignupForm>::from_request(req)?;
// Returns 400 on extraction failure, 422 with JSON error body on validation failure.
```

---

## Session management (src/session/mod.rs)

```rust
use rust_web_server::session::{SessionStore, session_id_from_request, session_cookie, destroy_cookie};

// Store in AppState:
struct State { sessions: SessionStore }
let state = State { sessions: SessionStore::new(3600) }; // TTL in seconds

// Create a session:
let mut session = state.sessions.create();
session.set("user_id", "42");
state.sessions.save(&session);
// Set cookie on response:
response.headers.push(Header {
    name: "Set-Cookie".to_string(),
    value: session_cookie(&session.id, "sid", 3600),
});

// Load a session:
let sid = session_id_from_request(request, "sid")?;
let session = state.sessions.load(&sid)?;
let user_id = session.get("user_id")?;

// Destroy:
state.sessions.destroy(&sid);
response.headers.push(Header {
    name: "Set-Cookie".to_string(),
    value: destroy_cookie("sid"),
});
```

`Session.id` is a public `String` field.

**Persistent sessions â€” DbSessionStore** (requires `model-sqlite` / `model-postgres` / `model-mysql`):

```rust
use rust_web_server::model::DbPool;
use rust_web_server::session::DbSessionStore;

let pool = DbPool::new(config).await?;     // or DbPool::memory().await for SQLite
let store = DbSessionStore::new(pool, 3600).await?;  // auto-creates rws_sessions table

let mut sess = store.create().await?;      // all methods are async fn
sess.set("user_id", "42");
store.save(&sess).await?;

let loaded = store.load(&sess.id)?.unwrap();
store.purge_expired()?;                    // DELETE WHERE expires_at <= now
```

**Persistent sessions â€” RedisSessionStore**:

```rust
use rust_web_server::session::RedisSessionStore;

// From args or from_env() (RWS_REDIS_HOST/PORT/PASSWORD/TTL_SECS):
let store = RedisSessionStore::new("127.0.0.1:6379", None, 3600);

let mut sess = store.create()?;            // returns io::Result
sess.set("role", "admin");
store.save(&sess)?;                        // SET rws:sess:{id} EX ttl
store.destroy(&sess.id)?;                  // DEL rws:sess:{id}
store.purge_expired();                     // no-op â€” Redis TTL handles it
```

---

## Auth middleware (src/auth/mod.rs, requires `auth` feature)

```rust
use rust_web_server::auth::{BasicAuthLayer, JwtLayer, build_jwt, verify_jwt};

// Basic Auth â€” closure receives (username, password):
let app = App::new().wrap(BasicAuthLayer::new(|user, pass| user == "admin" && pass == "secret"));

// Basic Auth from an htpasswd-style file (loaded once at construction):
// lines are "user:password" (plain text) or "user:{SHA256}<base64>" (rws's own
// scheme â€” NOT Apache's {SHA}/SHA-1, $apr1$, or bcrypt; generate an entry with
// `printf '%s' 'pw' | openssl dgst -sha256 -binary | openssl base64`).
let app = App::new().wrap(BasicAuthLayer::from_htpasswd_file(".htpasswd")?);

// JWT HS256 (secret is `impl Into<Vec<u8>>` â€” a byte string or String, not &str):
let app = App::new().wrap(JwtLayer::new(b"my-secret-key"));

// Build a JWT â€” build_jwt(claims_json: &str, secret: &[u8]) -> String:
let token = build_jwt(r#"{"sub":"user-123","exp":9999999999}"#, b"my-secret-key");

// Verify manually â€” verify_jwt(token: &str, secret: &[u8]) -> Option<Claims>:
let claims = verify_jwt(&token, b"my-secret-key").unwrap();
// claims.sub: Option<String>, claims.exp: Option<u64>, claims.raw: String (raw JSON)
```

### RS256 / ES256 (requires `auth-asymmetric` feature)

Verifies JWTs signed by a separate auth server's RSA/P-256 private key, given only its public key â€” no JWKS endpoint, no full `sso` feature. Both `public_key_pem` args are SubjectPublicKeyInfo PEM (`-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----`), e.g. `openssl rsa -in key.pem -pubout` / `openssl ec -in key.pem -pubout`.

```rust
use rust_web_server::auth::{JwtLayer, verify_jwt_rs256, verify_jwt_es256};

let app = App::new().wrap(JwtLayer::rs256(&rsa_public_key_pem)?);
let app = App::new().wrap(JwtLayer::es256(&ec_public_key_pem)?);

// Standalone verify (same functions JwtLayer uses internally), for use in a handler:
let claims = verify_jwt_rs256(&token, &rsa_public_key).unwrap();
let claims = verify_jwt_es256(&token, &ec_public_key).unwrap();
```

Both reject a token whose header `alg` doesn't match what was actually verified â€” an RS256 key can't wave through a token whose header claims `HS256`. ES256 signatures are the raw 64-byte `r || s` JWTs specify, not ASN.1 DER. Use `sso::JwksCache` instead when the signing key rotates behind a live JWKS URL.

Config-driven proxy wiring for JWT/Basic â€” see "Config-driven proxy server" below.

---

## ForwardAuth middleware (src/auth/forward.rs, requires `auth` feature)

Delegates the allow/deny decision to an external HTTP service (Traefik `forwardAuth`, nginx `auth_request`) â€” for a centralized policy engine (OPA, Casbin) or a shared SSO/session service. No new deps: reuses `crate::http_client::Client`.

```rust
use rust_web_server::auth::forward::ForwardAuthLayer;

let app = App::new()
    .wrap(ForwardAuthLayer::new("http://auth.internal/verify")
        .copy_header("X-User-Id")   // copied from the auth response onto the forwarded request on 2xx
        .copy_header("X-Roles")
        .timeout_ms(2000));         // default 5000ms
```

Flow, per request:
1. Sends `GET <auth_url>` with every incoming request header copied onto it (redirects from the auth service are NOT followed â€” `max_redirects(0)` â€” so a 3xx from the auth service reaches step 3 as-is).
2. `2xx` â†’ request proceeds to `next`. Each `.copy_header(name)` value present on the *auth response* **replaces** (not appends alongside) any same-named header already on the forwarded request â€” this is what stops a client from smuggling a forged `X-User-Id` past the auth service's verified value.
3. Any other status â†’ the auth service's response is returned to the client **verbatim** (status code, headers minus hop-by-hop/framing ones, body) â€” preserves `WWW-Authenticate` challenges and OAuth `Location` redirects.
4. Auth service unreachable (connection refused, timeout, DNS failure) â†’ `502 Bad Gateway`. Fails closed.

---

## Webhook signature verification (src/webhook/mod.rs, requires `webhook` feature)

HMAC verification for the three most common inbound-webhook conventions, so handlers don't hand-wire `hmac`+`sha2` per integration. `body` must be the exact raw request bytes â€” a re-serialized `Json<T>` will not reproduce what the provider signed.

```rust
use rust_web_server::webhook::{verify_webhook_signature, WebhookProvider};

verify_webhook_signature(WebhookProvider::GitHub, body, secret, header_value);
// or call the provider-specific function directly:
use rust_web_server::webhook::{verify_github_signature, verify_shopify_signature, verify_stripe_signature};
verify_github_signature(body, secret, header_value);   // X-Hub-Signature-256: sha256=<hex>
verify_shopify_signature(body, secret, header_value);  // X-Shopify-Hmac-Sha256: <base64>
verify_stripe_signature(body, secret, header_value);   // Stripe-Signature: t=<unix_ts>,v1=<hex>[,v1=...]
```

Stripe's signed payload is `"{timestamp}.{body}"`; checked against a 300s replay-window by default (`STRIPE_DEFAULT_TOLERANCE_SECS`) â€” use `verify_stripe_signature_with_tolerance(body, secret, header_value, tolerance_secs)` to override it. Multiple `v1=` entries (secret rotation) pass if any one matches. GitHub's legacy SHA-1 `X-Hub-Signature` is intentionally not supported â€” only `X-Hub-Signature-256`.

---

## CSRF protection (src/csrf/mod.rs, requires `csrf` feature)

```rust
use rust_web_server::csrf::{CsrfLayer, CsrfToken};

// Middleware: validates POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE, passes GET/HEAD/OPTIONS through.
let app = App::new().wrap(CsrfLayer::new());

// In a GET handler â€” embed token in HTML form:
let token = CsrfToken::from_request(req).map(|t| t.value().to_string()).unwrap_or_default();
// <input type="hidden" name="_csrf" value="{token}">
// For AJAX: read cookie `_csrf` in JS and send as `X-CSRF-Token` header.

// Options:
CsrfLayer::new()
    .http_only(true)          // restrict to HTML forms (disables JS access)
    .secure(true)             // add Secure flag (use behind HTTPS)
    .cookie_name("xsrf")     // custom cookie name
    .header_name("X-XSRF-Token")
```

Double-submit cookie pattern: random 32-byte token, `SameSite=Strict`, constant-time comparison.

---

## Password hashing (src/crypto/mod.rs, requires `crypto` feature)

```rust
use rust_web_server::crypto::{hash_password, verify_password, generate_token};

let hash = hash_password("hunter2")?;     // Argon2id, random salt, returns PHC string
let ok   = verify_password("hunter2", &hash)?;  // constant-time, Ok(true/false)
let tok  = generate_token(32);            // 64-char lowercase hex from OS CSPRNG
```

Store the PHC string as-is â€” salt is embedded. Use `generate_token` for password-reset tokens, API keys, email verification codes.

---

## Signed and encrypted cookies (src/cookie/crypto_ext.rs, requires `crypto` feature)

`SetCookie` values are plain-text and forgeable by the client. These two function pairs cover tamper-evidence and confidentiality:

```rust
use rust_web_server::cookie::{signed_cookie, verify_signed_cookie, encrypted_cookie, decrypt_cookie, SetCookie};

// HMAC-SHA256 â€” value stays plain-text/readable by the client, but tampering
// (to the value or the signature) is detected. Format: "<value>.<hex-sig>".
let signed = signed_cookie("plan=pro", b"my-signing-secret");
let header_value = SetCookie::new("prefs", &signed).http_only().build();
let original = verify_signed_cookie(&signed, b"my-signing-secret"); // Some("plan=pro")
let tampered = verify_signed_cookie(&signed, b"wrong-secret");      // None

// AES-256-GCM â€” value is neither readable nor tamperable by the client.
// `key` can be any length (SHA-256-derived into the required 256-bit key).
// Format: "<hex-nonce>.<hex-ciphertext-and-tag>"; fresh random nonce per call.
let encrypted = encrypted_cookie("session-token-abc123", b"my-encryption-key");
let header_value = SetCookie::new("sess", &encrypted).http_only().secure().build();
let original = decrypt_cookie(&encrypted, b"my-encryption-key"); // Some("session-token-abc123")
```

Both verify/decrypt functions return `None` on any failure (missing separator, malformed hex, tampering, or the wrong secret/key) rather than a detailed error â€” avoids using error contents as an oracle. `verify_signed_cookie` splits on the *last* `.`, so a value containing dots still round-trips correctly.

---

## OAuth2 / OIDC SSO (src/sso/, requires `sso` feature)

```rust
use std::sync::Arc;
use rust_web_server::session::SessionStore;
use rust_web_server::sso::{OidcAuth, OidcConfig, OidcClaims};

let sessions = Arc::new(SessionStore::new(86_400));

let app = App::new()
    .wrap(
        OidcAuth::new(
            OidcConfig::google(client_id, client_secret, "https://myapp.com/auth/callback"),
            Arc::clone(&sessions),
        )
        .exclude("/healthz")
        .exclude("/public/"),
    );

// OidcAuth intercepts:
//   GET /auth/login    â†’ redirect to IdP with PKCE + state + nonce
//   GET /auth/callback â†’ exchange code, verify id_token, store claims in session
//   GET /auth/logout   â†’ destroy session
// All other paths require a valid session or are redirected to /auth/login.

// Inside any protected handler:
fn dashboard(req: &Request, conn: &ConnectionInfo) -> Response {
    let claims: OidcClaims = OidcAuth::claims(req).unwrap();
    // claims.sub, claims.email, claims.name, claims.picture, â€¦
}

// Load config from env (RWS_OIDC_PROVIDER, RWS_OIDC_CLIENT_ID, â€¦):
let config = OidcConfig::from_env()?;

// Provider presets:
OidcConfig::google(id, secret, uri)
OidcConfig::microsoft("tenant-id", id, secret, uri)
OidcConfig::github(id, secret, uri)          // OAuth 2.0 only (no OIDC JWT)
OidcConfig::okta("company.okta.com", id, secret, uri)
OidcConfig::auth0("company.auth0.com", id, secret, uri)
OidcConfig::keycloak("https://kc.example.com", "my-realm", id, secret, uri)
OidcConfig::discover("https://idp.example.com", id, secret, uri)?  // fetches /.well-known/openid-configuration
```

### JWT verification standalone

```rust
use rust_web_server::sso::{JwksCache, VerifyOptions};

let cache = JwksCache::new("https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs");
let claims = cache.verify_jwt(&id_token, &VerifyOptions {
    audience:    "my-client-id.apps.googleusercontent.com",
    issuer:      "https://accounts.google.com",
    leeway_secs: 30,
})?;
// Supports RS256 (RSA) and ES256 (EC P-256). Auto-rotates keys on kid miss.
```

### `rws` as its own OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server (`sso-server` feature, implies `sso` + `auth`)

```rust
use std::sync::Arc;
use std::time::Duration;
use rust_web_server::session::SessionStore;
use rust_web_server::sso::server::{AuthServer, AuthServerConfig};
use rust_web_server::sso::client_store::{ClientStore, OAuthClient, GrantType};

let auth_server = AuthServer::new(AuthServerConfig {
    issuer:            "https://myapp.com".into(),
    signing_secret:    std::env::var("RWS_AUTH_SIGNING_SECRET")?,
    access_token_ttl:  Duration::from_secs(3600),
    refresh_token_ttl: Duration::from_secs(86_400 * 30),
    clients: ClientStore::new().add(OAuthClient {
        client_id:     "backend-service".into(),
        client_secret: Some("s3cr3t".into()),
        redirect_uris: vec![],
        grants:        vec![GrantType::ClientCredentials],
        scopes:        vec!["api:read".into()],
    }),
    sessions: Arc::new(SessionStore::new(86_400)),
});
let app = App::new().wrap(auth_server);
// Intercepts: POST /oauth/token (client_credentials | authorization_code+PKCE | refresh_token),
//             GET /oauth/authorize, GET /.well-known/openid-configuration, GET /.well-known/jwks.json
```

Two deliberate deviations from a textbook Authorization Server (both documented in `src/sso/server.rs`):
tokens are signed HS256 via the existing `auth::build_jwt`, not an RSA/EC PEM key (this crate has no
private-key PEM/DER parser) â€” so `/.well-known/jwks.json` always returns `{"keys":[]}`; configure
resource servers with the same `signing_secret` directly. And `/oauth/authorize` has no built-in login
page â€” it trusts an existing app session (default key `"user_id"`, override with
`.subject_session_key()`), redirecting to a configurable `login_url` (default `/login`) if absent.
`ClientStore` is code/builder-registered only (`.add(...)`) â€” no `::from_env()`, since a list of
per-client secrets has no clean flat-env-var shape.

## SAML 2.0 Service Provider (src/sso/saml/, `sso-saml` feature, implies `sso`)

```rust
use std::sync::Arc;
use rust_web_server::session::SessionStore;
use rust_web_server::sso::saml::{SamlSp, SamlConfig, SamlIdpMetadata, AttributeMap};

let saml_sp = SamlSp::new(SamlConfig {
    sp_entity_id: "https://myapp.com/saml/metadata".into(),
    sp_acs_url:   "https://myapp.com/saml/acs".into(),
    idp_metadata: SamlIdpMetadata::from_file("idp-metadata.xml")?,
    sessions:     Arc::new(SessionStore::new(86_400)),
})
.attribute_map(AttributeMap::new()
    .map("http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/emailaddress", "email"));

let app = App::new().wrap(saml_sp);
// Intercepts: GET /saml/metadata, GET /saml/login, POST /saml/acs, GET /saml/logout
```

`/saml/acs` verifies RSA-SHA256 XML signatures, requires exactly one `Assertion` (signature-wrapping
mitigation), and checks `Conditions`/`AudienceRestriction`/`InResponseTo`/`SubjectConfirmation` per
spec. Four documented deviations (all in `src/sso/saml/mod.rs`'s module docs): no `quick-xml` dep (a
small hand-rolled XML parser is used instead, rejecting `<!DOCTYPE>` outright â€” no XXE surface);
signature verification is byte-exact against the transmitted document, not full XML C14N (fail-closed,
not fail-open); `AuthnRequest`s use the HTTP-POST binding, unsigned (no DEFLATE dependency, no
private-key parser); logout is local-only (no IdP `LogoutRequest`, mirroring `OidcAuth`). `AttributeMap`
maps into `SamlClaims { name_id, attributes }`, not `OidcClaims` (which has no `groups` field).

---

## Email / SMTP (src/mailer/mod.rs, requires `mailer` feature)

STARTTLS and SMTPS additionally require `http-client` or `http2`. Plain SMTP (`SmtpTls::None`) works with only `mailer`.

```rust
use rust_web_server::mailer::{Email, Mailer, SmtpTls};

// From environment variables (RWS_SMTP_HOST, RWS_SMTP_FROM, etc.)
let mailer = Mailer::from_env()?;

// Direct construction
let mailer = Mailer {
    host: "smtp.gmail.com".into(),
    port: 587,
    user: Some("you@gmail.com".into()),
    password: Some("app-password".into()),
    from: "you@gmail.com".into(),
    tls: SmtpTls::Starttls,   // Starttls | Smtps | None
    timeout_ms: 10_000,
};

// Build and send an email
let email = Email::builder()
    .to("user@example.com")
    .cc("team@example.com")        // optional
    .subject("Welcome!")
    .text("Thanks for signing up.") // plain text
    .html("<p>Thanks for <b>signing up</b>.</p>") // HTML (multipart if both set)
    .reply_to("support@example.com")
    .build()?;

mailer.send(&email)?;
```

| Env variable | Default | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `RWS_SMTP_HOST` | required | SMTP server hostname |
| `RWS_SMTP_PORT` | `587` | 25=relay, 587=STARTTLS, 465=SMTPS |
| `RWS_SMTP_USER` | â€” | Omit to skip AUTH |
| `RWS_SMTP_PASSWORD` | â€” | |
| `RWS_SMTP_FROM` | required | Envelope + `From:` address |
| `RWS_SMTP_TLS` | `starttls` | `starttls` \| `smtps` \| `none` |
| `RWS_SMTP_TIMEOUT_MS` | `10000` | Connect/read/write timeout ms |

`Email::builder()` validates: at least one `to`, non-empty `subject`, at least one of `text`/`html`. Generates `multipart/alternative` when both text and HTML are provided. SMTP dot-stuffing applied automatically (RFC 5321 Â§4.5.2).

---

## Outbound HTTP client (src/http_client/, always available)

```rust
use rust_web_server::http_client::{Client, HttpClientError};

// Plain HTTP (no feature flag needed):
let resp = Client::new()
    .get("http://api.example.com/data")
    .header("Authorization", "Bearer tok")
    .timeout_ms(5_000)
    .send()?;
assert!(resp.is_success());
let body: String = resp.text()?;

// HTTPS (requires `http-client` or `http2` feature):
let resp = Client::new().post("https://api.example.com/charge")
    .body_json(r#"{"amount":1000}"#)
    .send()?;

// Form-urlencoded body (OAuth token / JWKS-fronting IdP calls, etc.):
let resp = Client::new().post("https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token")
    .form(&[("grant_type", "authorization_code"), ("code", "abc123")])
    .send()?;

// Async (requires `http2` feature):
use rust_web_server::http_client::AsyncClient;
let resp = AsyncClient::new().get("https://api.example.com").send().await?;
```

Follows redirects automatically. GET-downgrade on 301/302/303; method-preserve on 307/308.
`.form(&[(k,v)])` (on both `RequestBuilder` and `AsyncRequestBuilder`) percent-encodes each pair
and sets `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` â€” used internally by
`sso::client::OidcClient::exchange_code` for the authorization-code token exchange.

---

## Model layer / ORM (src/model/, requires `model-sqlite` / `model-postgres` / `model-mysql`)

All model features imply `http2` (tokio runtime). The driver is `sqlx`; `DbPool` wraps `sqlx::Pool` and is cheap to clone. The three `model-*` features are **not** mutually exclusive â€” enable more than one to hold a `DbPool` to each backend in the same binary; `Backend` (`Sqlite`/`Postgres`/`MySql`, one variant per compiled-in feature) selects which one a given `DbPool`/`DbConfig` talks to.

```rust
use rust_web_server::model::{DbPool, DbConfig};

#[derive(Model)]  // requires macros feature
#[table(name = "users")]
struct User {
    #[primary_key(auto_increment)]
    pub id:    i64,
    pub name:  String,
    pub email: String,
}

let pool = DbPool::new(DbConfig::from_env()?).await?;

// Migration:
pool.migrate("migrations/").await?;
// Rollback (needs a companion `.down.sql` per up file, e.g. 0001_x.sql / 0001_x.down.sql):
pool.rollback_last("migrations/").await?;   // -> Option<String>, undoes the most recent
pool.rollback("migrations/", 3).await?;     // -> Vec<String>, undoes up to the last 3

// CRUD (all async):
let repo = User::repository(&pool);
let user = repo.find_by_id(1).await?;
repo.save(&User { id: 0, name: "Alice".into(), email: "a@b.com".into() }).await?;
repo.delete_by_id(1).await?;
let all = repo.find_all().await?;

// Query builder (terminal methods are async):
let adults: Vec<User> = User::query(&pool)
    .filter("age >= ?", vec![Value::Int(18)])
    .order_by("name", Order::Asc)
    .limit(10)
    .fetch_all().await?;

// Transactions:
pool.transaction(|mut tx| async move {
    tx.execute("INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)", &[...]).await?;
    tx.commit().await
}).await?;

// Pagination â€” offset-based (Page<T>, with total_items/total_pages):
use rust_web_server::model::Page;
let page: Page<User> = User::query(&pool).order_by("id", Order::Asc).paginate(2, 20).await?;
// page.items, page.total_items, page.total_pages, page.has_next(), page.has_prev()

// Pagination â€” cursor/keyset-based (CursorPage<T>, no COUNT(*), forward-only):
use rust_web_server::model::CursorPage;
let page: CursorPage<User> = User::query(&pool).paginate_after(None, 20).await?;
let next: CursorPage<User> = User::query(&pool).paginate_after(page.next_cursor.as_deref(), 20).await?;

// Both build an RFC 8288 Link header:
let link = page.link_header("https://api.example.com/users", "cursor"); // CursorPage
// Page::link_header(base_url) instead builds rel="first"/"prev"/"next"/"last"
```

`DbConfig::from_env()` reads `RWS_DB_HOST`, `RWS_DB_PORT`, `RWS_DB_USER`, `RWS_DB_PASSWORD`, `RWS_DB_NAME`, `RWS_DB_POOL_SIZE`, plus `RWS_DB_BACKEND` ("sqlite"/"postgres"/"mysql") â€” inferred automatically if only one `model-*` feature is compiled in, **required** if more than one is (errors otherwise).

**Multiple backends in one binary** (e.g. `features = ["model-sqlite", "model-postgres"]`):

```rust
use rust_web_server::model::{Backend, DbConfig, DbPool};

let hot = DbPool::new(DbConfig { backend: Backend::Sqlite, database: "hot.db".into(), pool_size: 5, host: String::new(), port: 0, user: String::new(), password: String::new() }).await?;
let analytics = DbPool::new(DbConfig { backend: Backend::Postgres, host: "db.internal".into(), port: 5432, user: "app".into(), password: "secret".into(), database: "analytics".into(), pool_size: 10 }).await?;
```

`Page<T>`/`CursorPage<T>` live in `src/pagination/mod.rs` â€” no feature gate, no dependency on the model layer; construct one directly with `Page::new(items, page, per_page, total_items)` if your data source isn't `QueryBuilder`.

**SQLite in-memory pool (model-sqlite only):**

```rust
// Each call is a fresh isolated in-memory database â€” ideal for tests:
let pool = DbPool::memory().await?;
```

Relationships: `HasMany<T>`, `HasOne<T>`, `BelongsTo<O>` â€” explicit `.load(&pool).await`, no lazy loading, no hidden N+1.

---

## Middleware reference

| Type | Module | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `RateLimitLayer` | `middleware` | Per-IP sliding-window; reads `RWS_CONFIG_RATE_LIMIT_*`; 429 on exceeded |
| `BasicAuthLayer<F>` | `auth` | HTTP Basic; closure `(user, pass) -> bool`; 401 on failure. `::from_htpasswd_file(path)` loads plain-text/`{SHA256}` entries from a file. |
| `JwtLayer` | `auth` | HS256 Bearer token; 401 on invalid. `::rs256`/`::es256` (RS256/ES256 against a static PEM public key) require `auth-asymmetric` |
| `ForwardAuthLayer` | `auth::forward` | Delegates the allow/deny decision to an external HTTP service; 2xx copies configured headers through, other statuses pass the auth response through verbatim, unreachable â†’ 502 |
| `IpFilter` | `ip_filter` | Allow or deny by IPv4/CIDR; 403 on block |
| `CsrfLayer` | `csrf` | Double-submit cookie; 403 on mismatch for POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE |
| `OidcAuth` | `sso` | OAuth2/OIDC auth; redirects unauthenticated users to `/auth/login` |
| `AuthServer` | `sso-server` | `rws` as its own OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server; intercepts `/oauth/token`, `/oauth/authorize`, `/.well-known/*`; HS256 tokens |
| `SamlSp` | `sso-saml` | SAML 2.0 Service Provider; intercepts `/saml/{metadata,login,acs,logout}`; RSA-SHA256 XML signature verification |
| `MetricsLayer` | `metrics` | Per-route Prometheus counters + histograms |
| `OtelLayer` / `otel::span` / `otel::client_span` | `otel` | W3C traceparent root span + nested child spans; exports to stdout or OTLP |
| `CacheLayer` | `cache` | In-memory GET response cache; builder `.ttl(secs)` |
| `ReverseProxy` | `proxy` | HTTP/1.1 upstream proxy; round-robin; 502 on all-fail; built-in `ConnPool`; SSE + chunked AI streams + large downloads forwarded without buffering via `Response::stream_pipe`; `.with_circuit_breaker(Arc<dyn Breaker>)` auto-wires a `CircuitBreaker`/`RedisCircuitBreaker` â€” skips `Open` backends, records outcomes automatically |
| `ConnPool` | `proxy` | Per-backend HTTP/1.1 keep-alive connection pool; `new(max_idle, timeout)` |
| `H2ReverseProxy` | `proxy` | HTTP/2 upstream proxy; `h2://` plain TCP, `h2s://`/`https://` TLS (ALPN `h2`); port defaults to 443 for TLS schemes; async-native sync/async bridge (works under any tokio runtime flavor); `http2` feature |
| `GrpcProxy` | `proxy` | gRPC proxy; filters on `Content-Type: application/grpc*`; `grpc://` plain, `grpcs://`/`https://` TLS; `http2` feature |
| `RewriteLayer` | `rewrite` | Request/response header, URI, status, body rewriting. `.request_uri_regex_rewrite(pattern, replacement)` (requires `rewrite-regex`) â€” regex match against the URI, whole URI replaced by the `$1`/`${name}`-expanded replacement on match, no-op otherwise |
| `CanaryLayer` | `canary` | Weighted traffic splitting via `WeightedBackend::new(url, weight)`, smooth weighted round-robin (no bursts); backends may be plain HTTP or TLS (`https://`/`h2s://`/`grpcs://`, requires `http-client`/`http2`); `Clone` + `.update(backends, pools)` for live weight changes; `WeightedPool`/`.add_pool()` for `BackendPool`-sourced groups â€” see dedicated section below |
| `RetryLayer` | `circuit_breaker` | Retry on 502/503/504 up to `max_retries` |
| `Breaker` | `circuit_breaker` | Object-safe trait behind `ReverseProxy::with_circuit_breaker`; implemented for `Mutex<CircuitBreaker>`, `RedisCircuitBreaker`, and `&T where T: Breaker` |
| `OidcAuth` | `sso` | OIDC auth flow + claims injection; `.exclude(prefix)` |

---

## Canary / traffic splitting (src/canary/)

```rust
use rust_web_server::app::App;
use rust_web_server::core::New;
use rust_web_server::canary::{CanaryLayer, WeightedBackend, WeightedPool};
use rust_web_server::middleware::WithMiddleware;
use rust_web_server::service_discovery::BackendPool;

// Fixed weighted backends â€” smooth weighted round-robin, not a flat
// pre-expanded rotation, so skewed weights (e.g. 9:1) never burst.
let layer = CanaryLayer::new(vec![
    WeightedBackend::new("stable-backend:8080", 9),
    WeightedBackend::new("canary-backend:8080", 1),
]);

// Clone before wrapping to keep a live-control handle.
let handle = layer.clone();
let app = WithMiddleware::new(App::new()).wrap(layer);

// Change weights at runtime â€” no restart, every clone (including the one
// already wrapped and serving traffic) picks it up on its next request.
handle.update(
    vec![
        WeightedBackend::new("stable-backend:8080", 1),
        WeightedBackend::new("canary-backend:8080", 1),
    ],
    vec![],
);

// A group's members can come from a BackendPool instead of a fixed URL â€”
// the weight controls how often the group is picked; which live member
// answers is a plain round-robin over pool.backends() at selection time.
let canary_pool = BackendPool::dns("canary.internal", 8080).poll_interval_secs(15);
canary_pool.start();
let app2 = App::new().wrap(
    CanaryLayer::new(vec![WeightedBackend::new("stable-backend:8080", 9)])
        .add_pool(canary_pool, 1),
);
// Or purely dynamic groups on both sides:
// CanaryLayer::with_pools(vec![WeightedPool::new(pool_a, 9), WeightedPool::new(pool_b, 1)]);
```

The SWRR algorithm (same one nginx uses): every entry's `current_weight` accumulates its configured
weight on each selection; the maximum is picked and then decremented by the total weight. Weights
`5, 1, 1` select roughly `A A B A C A A` (repeating) â€” never five `A`s in a row, which a flat
pre-expanded rotation list (the old implementation) would have produced. A request's failover across
multiple backends only perturbs this shared state once per request (the initial tick) â€” the fallback
order for that one request is a read-only ranking of the rest, so trying several backends doesn't
skew the sequence subsequent unrelated requests see.

`BackendPool` addresses are bare `"host:port"` with no scheme, so pool-sourced targets are always
plain HTTP/1.1 â€” mix in a TLS `WeightedBackend` group alongside a pool if a fixed TLS endpoint is
also needed. `weight = 0` (on either a `WeightedBackend` or `WeightedPool`) removes that entry from
selection entirely, whether set at construction or via `.update()`.

---

## Circuit breaker (src/circuit_breaker/)

```rust
use rust_web_server::circuit_breaker::{CircuitBreaker, global as global_breaker};

// In-process (resets on restart): global singleton (threshold=5, recovery=30s)
let mut cb = global_breaker().lock().unwrap();
cb.record_failure("backend-1:8080");
if cb.is_available("backend-1:8080") { /* send request */ }
cb.record_success("backend-1:8080");

// Or CircuitBreaker::new(failure_threshold, recovery_secs) for a custom instance.
```

Persisted across restarts and shared across `rws` instances â€” `RedisCircuitBreaker`, same method names, backed by Redis (hand-rolled RESP client, no feature gate, no new dependency):

```rust
use rust_web_server::circuit_breaker::RedisCircuitBreaker;

let cb = RedisCircuitBreaker::new("127.0.0.1:6379", None, 5, 30);
// or RedisCircuitBreaker::from_env() â€” RWS_REDIS_HOST/PORT/PASSWORD +
// RWS_CONFIG_CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD/RECOVERY_SECS

cb.record_failure("backend-1:8080")?;      // every method is Result<_, io::Error> â€” network I/O
if cb.is_available("backend-1:8080")? { /* send request */ }
cb.record_success("backend-1:8080")?;
```

**Automatic `ReverseProxy` wiring** â€” `.with_circuit_breaker(Arc<dyn Breaker>)` replaces manual `is_available`/`record_success`/`record_failure` calls around each proxied call: an `Open` backend is skipped with no dial attempt, and every outcome is recorded automatically.

```rust
use std::sync::Arc;
use rust_web_server::circuit_breaker::global as global_breaker;
use rust_web_server::proxy::ReverseProxy;

let proxy = ReverseProxy::new(["http://backend-1:8080", "http://backend-2:8080"])
    .with_circuit_breaker(Arc::new(global_breaker()));
```

`Breaker` is a small object-safe trait implemented for both `Mutex<CircuitBreaker>` and `RedisCircuitBreaker` (plus `&'static Mutex<CircuitBreaker>` via a blanket `impl<T: Breaker> Breaker for &T`, so `Arc::new(global_breaker())` â€” a `&'static` reference â€” works directly). `RedisCircuitBreaker`'s `Err` (Redis unreachable) is treated as *available* through this trait â€” fail open, not closed. No breaker wired by default â€” existing `ReverseProxy` code is unaffected unless it opts in.

**HalfOpen concurrency cap** â€” both breakers now cap how many concurrent probes are let through while `HalfOpen` (default `1`, was unbounded): `CircuitBreaker::new(t, r).max_half_open_probes(n)` (chainable) or `RedisCircuitBreaker::set_max_half_open_probes(n)` (live setter) / `RWS_CONFIG_CIRCUIT_BREAKER_MAX_HALF_OPEN_PROBES` env var. Without this, a burst of concurrent requests arriving the instant a backend transitions to `HalfOpen` would *all* count as "the" trial request â€” defeating the point of testing with one probe.

**Metric** â€” `GET /metrics` includes `rws_circuit_breaker_state{backend}` (gauge: `0`=closed, `1`=half_open, `2`=open) automatically, for every backend known to `circuit_breaker::global()`. Powered by `CircuitBreaker::all_states()`. No `RedisCircuitBreaker` equivalent â€” the minimal hand-rolled RESP client has no `SCAN`/`KEYS` support to enumerate its keys.

Each backend's state (`state|failures|opened_at`) is one Redis string key (`GET`/`SET`, not a hash) â€” not atomic across concurrent instances racing the same backend, unlike `RedisRateLimiter`'s `INCR` (a circuit breaker is a self-healing heuristic, not a hard resource boundary, so an occasional lost update doesn't matter).

`RetryLayer` (the middleware, see table above) does not consult either breaker automatically â€” call `is_available`/`record_success`/`record_failure` yourself in a custom controller/middleware before forwarding.

---

## Service discovery (src/service_discovery/)

```rust
use rust_web_server::service_discovery::BackendPool;

let pool = BackendPool::r#static(vec!["10.0.0.1:8080".into()]);          // fixed list, no polling
let pool = BackendPool::env_prefix("RWS_BACKENDS").poll_interval_secs(60); // RWS_BACKENDS_0, _1, â€¦
let pool = BackendPool::file("/etc/rws/backends.txt").poll_interval_secs(30); // one host:port per line
let pool = BackendPool::dns("my-service.internal", 8080).poll_interval_secs(15); // A-record lookup
let pool = BackendPool::dns_srv("_http._tcp.my-svc.default.svc.cluster.local").poll_interval_secs(15); // SRV lookup
let pool = BackendPool::consul("127.0.0.1:8500", "api").poll_interval_secs(10); // Consul health API
let pool = BackendPool::docker("rws.backend").poll_interval_secs(10);    // Docker label value = address
let pool = BackendPool::etcd(vec!["127.0.0.1:2379".into()], "/services/api/"); // etcd v3 watch stream

pool.start(); // background refresh (no-op for Static); dedicated watch thread for etcd
let backends: Vec<String> = pool.backends();
```

`DnsSrv` keeps only the lowest-priority tier of SRV answers (RFC 2782: clients try that tier first),
then repeats each `target:port` `weight.clamp(1, 20)` times so a flat round-robin `Vec<String>`
consumer still favors higher-weight targets proportionally â€” this is "weighted DNS": SRV carries a
`weight` field plain A records don't. `Consul` calls a Consul agent's `/v1/health/service/:name`
endpoint, which already applies health-check filtering server-side (`passing=true`); `Service.Address`
is preferred, falling back to `Node.Address` when a service registers without one. `Docker` queries the
local Docker Engine API over its Unix socket (`/var/run/docker.sock` by default,
`docker_with_socket(label, path)` overrides it) for running containers carrying `label`, using the
*label's value* as the backend address directly (e.g. `rws.backend=10.0.0.5:8080`) rather than guessing
an address from published ports or network topology â€” Unix-only, a logged no-op elsewhere.

`EtcdWatch` is the one source `BackendPool::start()` doesn't drive through the generic sleep-and-poll
loop: after an initial one-shot `/v3/kv/range` listing (so the pool works even without calling
`start()`), a dedicated thread holds a long-lived connection to etcd's gRPC-gateway `/v3/watch`
endpoint and applies `PUT`/`DELETE` events to the backend list incrementally as they arrive â€” no
polling interval after that. It reuses `crate::ingress::watch::read_chunked_lines`, the same
chunked-NDJSON-stream reader the Kubernetes Ingress watcher below already implements (the two
protocols shape their event streams the same way). Unlike the Ingress watcher â€” which treats every
event as a plain "something changed, re-list" trigger, since a `WatchEvent` there doesn't carry enough
to update one cached object in isolation â€” an etcd watch event *does* carry a complete key+value, so
it's applied as a real incremental delta against a local map. Plain HTTP only, no TLS yet.

Consul/Docker/etcd response JSON is parsed by a small hand-rolled recursive-descent value parser
(`src/service_discovery/json_lite.rs`, crate-internal) rather than the optional `serde`/`serde_json`
feature â€” `service_discovery` has no feature gate of its own and must stay usable in every build. DNS
SRV support (`src/service_discovery/dns_srv.rs`) is a from-scratch RFC 1035/2782 query/response codec
over a raw `UdpSocket` (including compression-pointer decoding) â€” no third-party DNS crate.

---

## Kubernetes Ingress (src/ingress/)

```rust
use rust_web_server::ingress::{IngressRouter, KubernetesIngressWatcher};

// kubectl proxy + env vars (RWS_K8S_API_SERVER/RWS_K8S_TOKEN/RWS_K8S_NAMESPACE):
let watcher = KubernetesIngressWatcher::from_env()?
    .ingress_class("rws"); // optional: only watch Ingress objects meant for this controller
watcher.start(); // periodic resync (default 30s) + a `?watch=true` connection for low-latency updates
let app = IngressRouter::new(watcher);

// Or, from inside a pod, no kubectl proxy sidecar (requires http-client or http2 feature):
let watcher = KubernetesIngressWatcher::from_service_account()?;
```

`from_service_account()` connects directly to `https://kubernetes.default.svc` over TLS, reading the
token/namespace/CA cert from the mounted service account files and the API server address from the
`KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST`/`KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT` env vars every pod has injected automatically.
Trusts only the cluster's own CA â€” not the public root store `http_client` uses elsewhere in this
crate â€” since the API server's certificate is signed by that private CA. Without `http-client`/`http2`,
it returns a clear error pointing back at `kubectl proxy` + `from_env()`.

`start()` spawns two background threads: the original interval-based resync, and (new) a long-lived
`?watch=true` streaming connection â€” any `ADDED`/`MODIFIED`/`DELETED` event triggers an immediate
re-list rather than being applied as an incremental delta (a deliberate scope decision â€” see
`src/ingress/watch.rs`'s module docs). A quiet cluster costs nothing (the watch thread just blocks on
a read); a real change is picked up as soon as the API server sends the event, not after the next
poll interval.

`IngressRule.path_type` (`Prefix`/`Exact`/`ImplementationSpecific`, parsed from `pathType`) is matched
per Kubernetes' real semantics: `Prefix` (the default, and `ImplementationSpecific`'s fallback) matches
whole path segments â€” `/foo` matches `/foo`, `/foo/`, `/foo/bar`, **not** `/foobar` â€” and `Exact`
requires full equality, ignoring any query string either way. `.ingress_class(name)` restricts to
Ingress objects whose `spec.ingressClassName` equals `name` exactly (unset = accept every class,
the historical default â€” needed on a multi-controller cluster running `rws` alongside e.g.
`nginx-ingress`).

---

## Observability

### Prometheus metrics

Built-in `GET /metrics` exposes counters. `MetricsLayer` adds per-route metrics.
`rws_circuit_breaker_state{backend}` (0=closed/1=half_open/2=open) is included automatically for
every backend `circuit_breaker::global()` knows about â€” no opt-in layer needed.

```rust
use rust_web_server::metrics::{record_request, record_error, SERVER_READY};
```

### OpenTelemetry tracing

```rust
use rust_web_server::otel;

otel::setup_from_env(); // OTEL_SERVICE_NAME, OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT
let app = App::new().wrap(OtelLayer);
// At shutdown:
otel::shutdown();
```

Nested child spans (multi-span tracing): `otel::span("db.query")` (kind Internal) or
`otel::client_span("upstream.call")` (kind Client) start a span nested under whatever span is
currently active on the thread â€” shares its `trace_id`, `parent_span_id` = the enclosing span's
`span_id`. `.set_attribute(key, value)`, `.set_error()`/`.record_error(msg)`; drop or `.end()` to
record. `otel::current_traceparent()` reflects the innermost active span, for propagating into
outbound calls.

### Access log

Combined Log Format by default. `RWS_CONFIG_LOG_FORMAT=json` switches to structured JSON.

### Hot config reload

`SIGHUP` or `POST /admin/config/reload` re-reads `rws.config.toml` and applies
CORS, rate-limit, log-format changes live. On TLS builds, also rebuilds the
`TlsAcceptor` with fresh certificates.

---

## Configuration

Layered priority (lowest â†’ highest):
1. Hardcoded defaults in `src/entry_point/mod.rs`
2. System environment variables
3. `rws.config.toml` in the working directory
4. Command-line args

Key environment variables:
```
RWS_CONFIG_IP                    default: 127.0.0.1
RWS_CONFIG_PORT                  default: 7878
RWS_CONFIG_THREAD_COUNT          default: number of CPU cores
RWS_CONFIG_TLS_CERT_FILE         path to PEM cert (enables TLS)
RWS_CONFIG_TLS_KEY_FILE          path to PEM key
RWS_CONFIG_TLS_CLIENT_CA_FILE    path to CA cert (enables mTLS)
RWS_CONFIG_HTTP_REDIRECT_PORT    if set, listens on this port and sends 301 â†’ HTTPS
RWS_CONFIG_RATE_LIMIT_MAX_REQUESTS   default: 1000
RWS_CONFIG_RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_SECS   default: 60
RWS_CONFIG_LOG_FORMAT            "combined" (default) | "json"
RWS_CONFIG_REQUEST_ALLOCATION_SIZE_IN_BYTES   default: 10000 (per-read chunk size; bodies larger than this span multiple reads)
RWS_CONFIG_MAX_BODY_SIZE_IN_BYTES   default: 0 (unlimited); 413 before buffering when exceeded
RWS_CONFIG_SPA_FALLBACK          default: unset (disabled); e.g. "index.html" â€” see "SPA fallback" section
RWS_CONFIG_SPA_FALLBACK_EXCLUDE_PREFIXES   default: unset; comma-separated, e.g. "/api,/healthz"
```

### Typed config binding (requires `macros` feature)

```rust
#[derive(Config)]
#[config(prefix = "APP_")]
struct AppConfig {
    #[config(env = "PORT", default = "8080")]
    port: u16,
    #[config(env = "DATABASE_URL")]
    database_url: Option<String>,
}
let cfg = AppConfig::load()?;
```

---

## Config-driven proxy server

When `rws.config.toml` contains `[[route]]` or `[[upstream]]` sections, the binary
automatically starts in proxy mode â€” no Rust code required.

```toml
[[upstream]]
name     = "api"
backends = ["10.0.0.10:8080", "10.0.0.11:8080"]
strategy = "least_connections"  # "round_robin" (default) | "random" | "ip_hash" | "least_connections"

  [upstream.health_check]
  path                = "/healthz"
  interval_secs       = 10
  healthy_threshold   = 2
  unhealthy_threshold = 3

[[route]]
  [route.match]
  host   = "api.example.com"
  path   = "/v1/*"
  method = "GET"

  [route.action]
  type = "proxy"

  [route.action.proxy]
  upstream = "api"

  [route.middleware]
  rate_limit = { max_requests = 500, window_secs = 60 }
  auth       = { type = "bearer", token_env = "API_TOKEN" }
  cache      = { ttl_secs = 3600 }

[[route]]
  [route.match]
  path = "/*"
  [route.action]
  type   = "respond"
  status = 404
  body   = "Not Found"
```

Route action types: `proxy`, `grpc`, `static`, `redirect`, `respond`, `mcp`.
Per-route middleware: `rate_limit`, `cache`, `auth`, `ip_allow`/`ip_deny`, `rewrite`, `timeout_ms`, `max_body_size`.
Upstream load-balancing strategies: `round_robin` (default), `random`, `ip_hash` (sticky per client IP), `least_connections`.

`auth` types (`[route.middleware.auth]`):
- `{ type = "bearer", token_env = "API_TOKEN" }` â€” static token, always available
- `{ type = "jwt", secret_env = "JWT_SECRET" }` â€” HS256 via `JwtLayer`, requires `auth` feature
- `{ type = "basic", htpasswd_file = ".htpasswd" }` â€” via `BasicAuthLayer::from_htpasswd_file`, requires `auth`
  feature; file supports plain-text and `{SHA256}` (rws's own scheme) entries only, not Apache's real
  `{SHA}`/`$apr1$`/bcrypt
- Unset/empty `secret_env`, a missing `htpasswd_file`, or no `auth` feature at build time all skip that
  route's auth check (warning logged to stderr at startup) rather than blocking every request

---

## MCP server (Model Context Protocol)

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpContent, McpServer};

let app = App::new().mcp("my-server", "1.0.0")
    .tool("add", "Add two numbers",
          r#"{"type":"object","properties":{"a":{"type":"number"},"b":{"type":"number"}}}"#,
          |args| {
              let a: f64 = rust_web_server::mcp::extract_arg(args, "a").unwrap_or_default().parse().unwrap_or(0.0);
              let b: f64 = rust_web_server::mcp::extract_arg(args, "b").unwrap_or_default().parse().unwrap_or(0.0);
              Ok(McpContent::text(format!("{}", a + b)))
          })
    .require_bearer("my-secret-token")  // optional static Bearer auth
    .wrap(App::new());                  // fall through non-MCP requests to inner app

// Serves POST /mcp (JSON-RPC 2.0, MCP Streamable HTTP transport).
// Built-in tools (when started as binary): server_config, feature_flags,
// server_metrics, rate_limit_config, check_rate_limit, cors_config,
// list_static_files, reload_config.
```

`initialize` negotiates the protocol version: it inspects the client's `params.protocolVersion` and
responds with the lower of that and the server's own version (`YYYY-MM-DD` date strings compare
correctly as plain strings), instead of always echoing back its own version regardless of what was
asked for. A client requesting a newer version than this server implements is told the version it
actually speaks; an older-version request is honored as sent. Missing `protocolVersion`/`params`
falls back to the server's version.

**Per-request context** â€” `.tool_with_context(name, desc, schema, |ctx, args| ...)` gives the handler
an `McpContext { client_name, client_version, session_id, auth_claims }`:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpContent, McpServer};

let app = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .tool_with_context("whoami", "Report the caller", "{}", |ctx, _args| {
        Ok(McpContent::text(format!("Called by {}", ctx.client_name.as_deref().unwrap_or("unknown"))))
    });
```

`initialize` mints a session id (same generator as `request_id::generate_request_id`), records that
session's `params.clientInfo`, and returns the id via an `Mcp-Session-Id` response header; the client
echoes it back on later requests so `execute()` can look the `clientInfo` back up and build the
`McpContext` a `tool_with_context` handler sees. `handle_request()` (bypassing `execute()`, e.g. in
tests) gives an empty `McpContext` â€” use `handle_request_with_context(body, ctx)` to supply one.
`auth_claims` is `None` unless `.require_oauth(...)` (MCP 2025-03-26 OAuth 2.0 authorization, `sso`
feature â€” see below) is configured and this request's bearer JWT verified successfully. Session
records accumulate for the process's lifetime with no eviction.

**Tool annotations (MCP 2025-03-26)** â€” `.tool_annotated(name, desc, schema, annotations, |args| ...)`
attaches behavioral hints clients use to decide whether to warn/confirm before calling a tool:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpContent, McpServer, ToolAnnotations};

let app = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .tool_annotated(
        "delete_file", "Delete a file from disk",
        r#"{"type":"object","properties":{"path":{"type":"string"}},"required":["path"]}"#,
        ToolAnnotations {
            destructive_hint: Some(true),
            read_only_hint: Some(false),
            idempotent_hint: Some(true),
            ..Default::default()
        },
        |_args| Ok(McpContent::text("deleted")),
    );
```

`ToolAnnotations` has four `Option<bool>` fields: `read_only_hint`, `destructive_hint`,
`idempotent_hint`, `open_world_hint`; unset ones default to `None`. Only `Some` fields serialize into
a `tools/list` entry's `annotations` object, using the spec's camelCase keys (`readOnlyHint`,
`destructiveHint`, `idempotentHint`, `openWorldHint`) â€” plain `.tool()`/`.tool_with_context()` tools
have no `annotations` key at all. Hints only â€” the server doesn't enforce them against handler
behavior. No builder combines annotations with `McpContext`; `.tool_annotated()`'s handler is
`Fn(&str) -> ...`, same shape as `.tool()`.

**Image and embedded-resource content** â€” `McpContent` gained two constructors beyond `text`/`json`:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::McpContent;

McpContent::image(base64_data, "image/png");
// â†’ {"type":"image","data":"<b64>","mimeType":"image/png"}

McpContent::embedded("docs://readme", "# My Project\n...", "text/markdown");
// â†’ {"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"docs://readme","mimeType":"text/markdown","text":"..."}}
```

`image(data, mime_type)` expects `data` already base64-encoded by the caller â€” no base64 encoder
ships in this crate. `embedded(uri, text, mime_type)` is for a resource included directly in a tool's
response, distinct from a client fetching it separately via `resources/read`. Both work anywhere an
`McpContent` is expected (tool results, prompt messages).

**JSON-RPC batch requests** â€” `POST /mcp` with a top-level JSON array instead of a single object
dispatches each element through the same method table as a standalone request, then joins the
non-notification results into one `[...]` array:

```json
// Request:
[{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/list","id":1},
 {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"ping","id":2}]

// Response â€” one entry per element, order preserved:
[{"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"tools":[...]},"id":1},
 {"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{},"id":2}]
```

Each element's success/error is independent â€” one erroring doesn't fail the batch or its siblings.
Elements with no `id` (notifications) contribute no entry, same as a standalone notification gets no
response body; an all-notification batch returns `202 Accepted` with no body. An empty array (`[]`)
is invalid per the JSON-RPC spec, so it gets back one `Invalid Request` error object, not `[]`. A
successful `initialize` inside a batch still mints a session and attaches `Mcp-Session-Id` to the
overall response (only the first `initialize` in a batch is honored for this, since one HTTP
response carries only one session id).

**Pagination for list methods** â€” `.page_size(n)` caps `tools/list`/`resources/list`/`prompts/list`
to `n` items per response:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::McpServer;

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0").page_size(50);
```

A partial response includes `"nextCursor"`, an opaque string echoed back as `params.cursor` to get
the next page:

```json
{"result":{"tools":[...50 items...],"nextCursor":"NTA="}}
```

Without `.page_size(...)`, every item comes back in one response and `nextCursor` never appears â€”
unchanged from before pagination existed. The cursor is base64 of a decimal offset but is meant to
be treated as opaque; a malformed/tampered cursor gets `INVALID_PARAMS` (`-32602`), and an offset
past the end returns an empty page with no `nextCursor` rather than erroring.

**SSE streaming transport (`GET /mcp`)** â€” a second transport alongside `POST /mcp`: `GET /mcp`
returns a `text/event-stream` response that stays open indefinitely for server â†’ client push.
`.notify(method, params_json)` broadcasts a JSON-RPC notification to every connected client from
anywhere in your code:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::McpServer;

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0");
server.notify("notifications/message", Some(r#"{"level":"info","data":"job finished"}"#));
```

Built on the existing `Response::stream_pipe` mechanism (already used for reverse-proxy passthrough
streaming), not a new response type: `GET /mcp` registers a bounded `mpsc::sync_channel(32)` sender,
returns a `Response` whose `stream_pipe` reads from that channel, and `Server::pipe_stream` (already
written, unmodified) drives it like any other streamed response. Idle connections get a `:
keep-alive` comment every 15s (also how a disconnected client gets detected/pruned); a client whose
buffer fills up (32 unread frames) is dropped the same way â€” `.notify()` never blocks on a slow
reader. HTTP/1.1 only â€” same scope as `stream_pipe` generally, since `h2_handler`/`h3_handler` don't
drive it for any response yet.

**`logging/setLevel` + `notifications/message`** â€” built on `.notify()` above. `.logging_enabled()`
advertises the `logging` capability in `initialize`; `.log(level, logger, data_json)` pushes a log
entry to every SSE-connected client, filtered by the level a client last set via `logging/setLevel`:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{LogLevel, McpServer};

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0").logging_enabled();
server.log(LogLevel::Warning, Some("database"), r#""connection pool exhausted""#);
```

`LogLevel` is the MCP spec's eight RFC 5424 severities, `Debug` through `Emergency` in declared
(and therefore `Ord`) order. Default minimum level (before any `logging/setLevel` call) is `Debug` â€”
nothing filtered. `.log()` inherits `.notify()`'s backpressure/disconnect handling; `.logging_enabled()`
only changes what's advertised, `.log()`/`logging/setLevel` work either way.

**Dynamic tool/resource/prompt registration + `listChanged`** â€” `.register_tool(...)`,
`.register_resource(...)`, `.register_prompt(...)` (and matching `.remove_tool(name)`,
`.remove_resource(uri_template)`, `.remove_prompt(name)`, each returning `bool`) take `&self`
instead of consuming the server, so they work from any thread holding a clone, at any time:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpContent, McpServer};

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0");
server.register_tool("refresh_cache", "Reload the cache", "{}", |_args| {
    Ok(McpContent::text("cache refreshed"))
});
let existed = server.remove_tool("refresh_cache"); // -> true
```

Backed by `Arc<RwLock<Vec<_>>>` (not a plain `Vec`) so every clone of `McpServer` shares one live
list. `do_tools_call`/`do_resources_read`/`do_prompts_get` clone the matched handler's `Arc` out of
a short read-lock before invoking it, so a slow handler never blocks a concurrent registration. Each
registration/removal that actually changes something pushes
`notifications/tools/list_changed`/`.../resources/list_changed`/`.../prompts/list_changed` (no
`params`) over SSE via `.notify()`; a no-op removal pushes nothing. `initialize` now advertises
`"listChanged":true` for all three unconditionally (`resources.subscribe` stays `false` â€” that's
TODO-14, not implemented). No dynamic equivalent of `.tool_with_context()`/`.tool_annotated()`/
`.prompt_with_args()` â€” remove and re-register under the same name to change those.

**`notifications/progress`** â€” if a `tools/call` request includes `params._meta.progressToken`,
`McpContext::report_progress(progress, total, message)` pushes a `notifications/progress` event
over SSE for that token (`.tool_with_context()` only, since a plain `.tool()` handler never sees
`McpContext`):

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpContent, McpServer};

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .tool_with_context("long_job", "Do something slow", "{}", |ctx, _args| {
        ctx.report_progress(0.0, Some(100.0), Some("starting"));
        ctx.report_progress(100.0, Some(100.0), Some("done"));
        Ok(McpContent::text("done"))
    });
```

Always safe to call â€” no-ops if the client sent no `progressToken`, or if `ctx` has no live server
behind it (e.g. `handle_request()`'s default empty context rather than `execute()`).
`progress_token` stores the raw JSON form (spec allows `string | number`), so it round-trips
correctly either way. Shares `render_notification`/`broadcast_sse_to` internally with `.notify()` â€”
`McpContext` just holds a clone of the broadcast list, not a whole `McpServer`.

**`completion/complete`** â€” `.completion(ref_type, ref_name, handler)` registers an
argument-autocomplete provider for a tool or prompt argument, for clients like Cursor/VS Code:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::McpServer;

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .completion("tool", "deploy", |arg_name, partial| {
        match arg_name {
            "region" => Ok(vec!["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"].into_iter()
                .filter(|r| r.starts_with(partial)).map(String::from).collect()),
            _ => Ok(vec![]),
        }
    });
```

`ref_type` is `"tool"`/`"prompt"`, matched against the request's `ref.type` (`"ref/tool"`/
`"ref/prompt"` on the wire) with the `"ref/"` prefix stripped. No match (unknown ref/name or
argument) returns empty `values`, not an error. More than 100 values from a handler are truncated
with `hasMore:true`/untruncated `total`, per spec guidance. `initialize` advertises
`"completions":{}` automatically once any `.completion()` is registered â€” no separate opt-in flag.
No dynamic (`&self`) equivalent â€” completions are builder-only, registered before serving requests.

**Request cancellation (`notifications/cancelled`)** â€” `ctx.is_cancelled()` in a
`.tool_with_context()` handler cooperatively checks whether the client asked to cancel this call:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpContent, McpServer};

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .tool_with_context("process_batch", "Process many items", "{}", |ctx, _args| {
        for i in 0..1_000_000 {
            if ctx.is_cancelled() { return Err("cancelled by client".to_string()); }
            // ... process item i ...
        }
        Ok(McpContent::text("done"))
    });
```

Cooperative, not preemptive â€” Rust can't forcibly interrupt a running sync closure (same limitation
`timeout::with_timeout` documents). A handler that never checks `is_cancelled()` runs to completion
regardless. Deliberately implemented as an `AtomicBool` flag rather than the original design's
`tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken` (async-only, gated on TODO-17's async tool handlers, which
don't exist yet) â€” no new dependency, works identically on `http1` and `http2`.
`cancellations: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, Arc<AtomicBool>>>>` keys a flag by the `tools/call`
request's raw `id`; `notifications/cancelled` flips the matching flag via `params.requestId` (raw
JSON token, `string | number` like `progressToken`). Unknown/finished request id: silent no-op.

**`resources/subscribe` + `resources/unsubscribe`** â€” `.notify_resource_updated(uri)` pushes
`notifications/resources/updated` over SSE, targeted at only the sessions subscribed to that URI
(unlike every other notification here, which broadcasts to all connected SSE clients):

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::McpServer;

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0");
server.notify_resource_updated("config://main"); // after the underlying data actually changes
```

Targeting requires knowing which `GET /mcp` SSE connection belongs to which session â€”
`start_sse_stream` now reads `Mcp-Session-Id` off the GET request and tags the client
(`SseClient { session_id, sender }`, replacing the old flat `Vec<SseSender>`);
`subscriptions: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, Vec<String>>>>` maps a URI to subscribed session ids;
`send_sse_to_sessions` (a session-filtered sibling of `broadcast_sse_to`) does the targeted push.
`resources/subscribe`/`resources/unsubscribe` both require `Mcp-Session-Id` â€” `INVALID_PARAMS`
without one, since there'd be no way to route the eventual update. `resources/unsubscribe` prunes a
URI's entry once its subscriber list is empty. `initialize`'s `resources` capability now advertises
`subscribe:true` unconditionally (was `false`).

Verified end-to-end with a real server: `initialize` â†’ `GET /mcp` with the returned session id â†’
`resources/subscribe` â†’ `.notify_resource_updated(...)` from another thread reached the subscribed
session's SSE stream; a second SSE connection with a different, unsubscribed session id got nothing.

**`sampling/createMessage` (server-side sampling)** â€” reverses the usual direction: the *server*
asks the client to run inference, over SSE, and the client answers via its own `POST /mcp`.
`ctx.sample(request, timeout)` in a `.tool_with_context()` handler sends the request and blocks:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpServer, PromptMessage, SamplingRequest};
use std::time::Duration;

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .tool_with_context("ask_llm", "Ask the connected client's model", "{}", |ctx, _args| {
        let response = ctx.sample(
            SamplingRequest { messages: vec![PromptMessage::user("2+2?")], max_tokens: 100, system_prompt: None },
            Duration::from_secs(30),
        )?;
        Ok(response.content)
    });
```

`SamplingRequest.messages` reuses `PromptMessage` (identical wire shape) instead of a near-duplicate
`SamplingMessage` type. `ctx.sample()` blocks the calling thread rather than being `async fn` â€” no
async tool handler support exists yet (TODO-17), so there's nothing to `.await` on anyway; a
thread-pool worker is tied up for up to `timeout`, same tradeoff `with_timeout` already accepts.
`McpServer` mints a request id, registers a oneshot `mpsc::Sender` for it in
`pending_replies: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, mpsc::Sender<Result<String,String>>>>>`, sends the
request via `send_sse_to_sessions`, and blocks on the matching receiver. The client's reply is a
JSON-RPC *response* (no `method`) â€” `handle_request_with_context`/`handle_batch` check a
method-less body with a recognized `id` against `pending_replies` before falling back to the old
"Missing method" error. Fails fast if the client's `initialize` never declared
`capabilities.sampling` (a `StoredClientInfo.supports_sampling`, since sampling is a *client*
capability, not server-advertised), if there's no session id, or if `ctx` has no live server;
times out if the client never answers. `sample()` and `list_roots()` below both wrap the same
shared `send_and_wait` mechanism â€” only the method/params sent and the `result` parsing differ.

Verified end-to-end with a real server: a `tools/call` invoking `ctx.sample(...)` blocked, the
`sampling/createMessage` request appeared on the session's SSE stream, and posting a response with
the matching id unblocked the original call with the sampled content.

**`roots/list` + `notifications/roots/list_changed`** â€” a client advertises which filesystem roots
it has access to, so file-system-aware tools stay within them. `ctx.list_roots(timeout)`:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::McpServer;
use std::time::Duration;

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .tool_with_context("list_workspace_files", "List client workspace files", "{}", |ctx, _args| {
        let roots = ctx.list_roots(Duration::from_secs(10))?;
        let names: Vec<String> = roots.iter().map(|r| r.uri.clone()).collect();
        Ok(rust_web_server::mcp::McpContent::text(names.join(", ")))
    });
```

Unlike `sample()`, results are **cached per session** â€” `StoredClientInfo.roots: Option<Vec<McpRoot>>`
(`None` = never fetched or invalidated). Only the first `list_roots()` call in a session sends
anything; later calls return the cache. `notifications/roots/list_changed` (special-cased ahead of
the generic notification branch, like `notifications/cancelled`) clears that session's cache via
`invalidate_roots_cache`, keyed by the notification's own `Mcp-Session-Id` (it carries no params â€”
just "this connection's cache is stale"). Fails fast the same way `sample()` does (translated to
roots); times out if the client never answers a live request.

Verified end-to-end with a real server: an initial `list_roots()` round-tripped and cached; a
second call returned instantly with no new SSE frame; `notifications/roots/list_changed` followed
by a third call produced a fresh `roots/list` request that resolved to newly-provided roots.

**Async tool handlers (`http2` feature)** â€” `.async_tool(name, desc, schema, handler)` registers a
tool whose handler is `async fn`, for work that naturally awaits (an `AsyncClient` call, an async DB
query) instead of blocking a thread inside a plain `.tool()` handler:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::{McpContent, McpServer};

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .async_tool("call_api", "Call an external API", "{}", |_args| async move {
        Ok(McpContent::text("response"))
    });
```

`tools/call` bridges into the future via `crate::async_bridge::block_on_isolated` â€” not
`tokio::task::block_in_place` (this feature's original design sketch), which panics outside a
`multi_thread` runtime. `block_on_isolated` (already used by `H2ReverseProxy`/`AsyncAppWithState`)
works under any runtime flavor. Storage is a separate `async_tools: Arc<RwLock<Vec<AsyncToolDef>>>`
(not unified into `ToolDef` via a handler enum); `tools/list` merges both collections transparently,
`tools/call` checks sync tools first then async. `.register_async_tool(...)` is the dynamic
equivalent; `.remove_tool(name)` now checks both collections. Like `.tool()`, no context/annotations
support yet for async tools.

Verified end-to-end against a real running `http2`/`http3` server (a live tokio runtime driving the
connection): an async tool awaiting `tokio::time::sleep(...)` took exactly as long as the sleep and
returned correctly, confirming the future is genuinely polled to completion, not just called.

**OAuth 2.0 authorization (MCP 2025-03-26, `sso` feature)** â€” `.require_oauth(provider, audience)` is
an alternative to `.require_bearer(token)`: instead of one static shared secret, it verifies a
client's bearer JWT against a live JWKS endpoint via `sso::jwks::JwksCache`:

```rust
use rust_web_server::mcp::McpServer;
use rust_web_server::sso::OidcProvider;

let server = McpServer::new("my-server", "1.0")
    .require_oauth(OidcProvider::google(), "my-mcp-client-id");
```

Same failure shape as `.require_bearer()` â€” missing/invalid token gets `401` with
`WWW-Authenticate: Bearer` before any JSON-RPC processing. On success, the verified claims
(a serialized `sso::OidcClaims`) populate `McpContext.auth_claims`, readable from any
`.tool_with_context()` handler. `GET /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` is served
automatically when `.require_oauth()` is configured (issuer/authorization_endpoint/token_endpoint/
jwks_uri from the `OidcProvider` â€” a minimal document, not full RFC 8414); it falls through to the
fallback app when OAuth isn't configured. If both `.require_bearer()` and `.require_oauth()` are set,
OAuth wins and the static token is never checked.

---

## Standalone proxies

```rust
// L4 TCP proxy:
```
