```rust
use rust_web_server::tcp_proxy::TcpProxy;
TcpProxy::new().backend("10.0.0.1:5432").backend("10.0.0.2:5432").bind("0.0.0.0:5432");

// UDP proxy:
use rust_web_server::udp_proxy::UdpProxy;
UdpProxy::new().backend("10.0.0.1:53").bind("0.0.0.0:53");

// WebSocket proxy (ws:// plain TCP, wss:// TLS â€” http-client or http2 feature):
use rust_web_server::ws_proxy::WsProxy;
WsProxy::new(["ws://chat-backend:9000"]).bind("0.0.0.0:8080");
WsProxy::new(["wss://chat.example.com"]).bind("0.0.0.0:8443"); // TLS, port defaults to 443

// WsProxy health checks (all backends always live by default â€” opt in):
use std::sync::{Arc, RwLock};
let all = vec!["wss://chat-a:8443".to_string(), "wss://chat-b:8443".to_string()];
let live = Arc::new(RwLock::new(all.clone()));
// ... spawn your own thread that writes the currently-healthy subset into `live` ...
WsProxy::with_live_backends(all, live).bind("0.0.0.0:8080"); // 503 if `live` is empty
// Or config-driven: add [ws_proxy.health_check] to rws.config.toml (same fields as
// [upstream.health_check]) and builder.rs wires this up automatically.
```

---

## Dependency injection (src/di/mod.rs)

```rust
use rust_web_server::di::Container;
use rust_web_server::app::App;

let mut container = Container::new();
container.register::<RedisClient>(RedisClient::new("redis://localhost"));
container.provide::<dyn Cache>(Arc::new(MemoryCache::new()));
container.register_named("primary_db", PgPool::new(â€¦));

// Pass the container itself as AppWithState's state â€” NOT container.into_arc().
// `with_state`/`with_async_state` already wrap `S` in an Arc internally, so
// `.into_arc()` first would double-wrap it (Arc<Arc<Container>>) for no benefit.
let app = App::with_state(container)
    .get("/x", |_req, _params, _conn, state| {
        let redis: Arc<RedisClient> = state.get::<RedisClient>().unwrap();
        let cache: Arc<dyn Cache>   = state.get::<dyn Cache>().unwrap();
        // ...
        Response::new()
    });
```

`Container::into_arc()` is for sharing one container across multiple hand-built `Application`s outside of `App::with_state`/`with_async_state`.

---

## Testing (src/test_client/mod.rs)

```rust
use rust_web_server::test_client::TestClient;
use rust_web_server::app::App;
use rust_web_server::core::New;

let client = TestClient::new(App::new());

let res = client.get("/healthz").send();
assert_eq!(200, res.status());

let res = client.post("/api/users")
    .header("Content-Type", "application/json")
    .body(r#"{"name":"Alice"}"#)
    .send();
assert_eq!(201, res.status());
```

`TestClient` dispatches requests directly through `Application::execute` â€” no TCP socket, no async runtime required.

### Isolated configuration (`ServerConfig` / `App::with_config`)

Tests that check CORS, CSP, or other header behavior should not write `RWS_CONFIG_*` env vars â€” that causes races under `cargo test --jobs`. Instead build a `ServerConfig` struct and pass it to `App::with_config`:

```rust
use rust_web_server::app::App;
use rust_web_server::server_config::ServerConfig;
use rust_web_server::test_client::TestClient;

// No env writes â†’ no test_env::lock() â†’ parallel-safe
let config = ServerConfig {
    cors_allow_all: false,
    cors_allow_origins: "https://trusted.example.com".to_string(),
    ..ServerConfig::default()
};
let client = TestClient::new(App::with_config(config));
let res = client.get("/").send();
```

`ServerConfig::from_env()` reads all `RWS_CONFIG_*` vars (used by `App::new()` on each request for hot-reload). `ServerConfig::default()` returns hardcoded defaults without touching env vars.

`AppWithState`, `AsyncAppWithState` (requires `http2`), and `ConfigDrivenApp` (config-driven proxy) each
fall through to a built-in `App` for anything their own routes/rules don't match. By default that
fallback reads env vars per request like `App::new()`; call `.with_config(ServerConfig)` on any of
them to pin it instead â€” same rationale, same parallel-test safety:

```rust
use rust_web_server::state::AppWithState;

let app = AppWithState::new(my_state)
    .with_config(config)
    .get("/version", version_handler);
```

---

## Background scheduler (src/scheduler/mod.rs)

```rust
use rust_web_server::scheduler::Scheduler;
use std::time::Duration;

Scheduler::new()
    .every(Duration::from_secs(60), || println!("tick"))               // fixed rate
    .after(Duration::from_secs(5), || println!("delayed once"))        // fixed delay
    .cron("0 0 * * * *", || println!("top of every hour"))             // 6-field cron
    .start();  // spawns daemon threads; returns immediately
```

---

## Background job queue (src/jobs/mod.rs, requires `jobs` feature)

One-shot, fire-and-forget work from handlers ("send this email after signup")
without blocking the response.

```rust
use rust_web_server::jobs::JobQueue;
use std::time::Duration;

let queue = JobQueue::new(4)              // 4 worker threads
    .max_retries(5)                       // retries after the first attempt (default: 3)
    .backoff(Duration::from_millis(200), 2); // initial backoff, multiplier (default: 500ms, 2x)

queue.submit(|| {
    // do the work; return Err(msg) to trigger a retry
    Ok(())
});
queue.join(); // drain + wait (tests/shutdown)
```

Named jobs implement the `Job` trait instead of a closure (`fn run(&self) -> Result<(), String>`,
`fn name(&self) -> &str` for retry/failure log lines).

`PersistentJobQueue` (additionally requires `model-sqlite`/`model-postgres`/`model-mysql`) persists
jobs to a `rws_jobs` table so they survive a crash/restart. Jobs are `(job_type, payload)` string
pairs â€” not closures â€” dispatched to a handler registered by name:

```rust,no_run
# async fn example() -> Result<(), rust_web_server::model::DbError> {
use rust_web_server::jobs::PersistentJobQueue;
use rust_web_server::model::DbPool;
use std::sync::Arc;

let pool = DbPool::from_env().await?;
let queue = Arc::new(PersistentJobQueue::new(pool).await?); // creates rws_jobs table; resets crashed `running` rows to `pending`

queue.register("send_welcome_email", |payload| {
    // send_welcome_email(payload)
    Ok(())
});
let _handles = Arc::clone(&queue).start(4); // 4 polling worker threads

queue.enqueue("send_welcome_email", "new-user@example.com").await?;
# Ok(())
# }
```

---

## File / object storage (src/storage/mod.rs)

`Storage` trait â€” write handler code once, swap the backend via config.

```rust
use rust_web_server::storage::{LocalStorage, Storage}; // requires `storage-local` feature

let store = LocalStorage::new("/var/data/uploads").with_base_url("/uploads");
let key = store.put("avatars/42.png", &file_bytes, "image/png")?;
let public_url = store.url(&key); // "/uploads/avatars/42.png"
```

```rust,no_run
use rust_web_server::storage::{S3Storage, Storage}; // requires `storage-s3` feature

// Reads RWS_S3_BUCKET, RWS_S3_REGION, and optionally RWS_S3_ENDPOINT (point at
// R2 / MinIO / any S3-compatible host). RWS_S3_ACCESS_KEY/SECRET_KEY are
// optional â€” when unset, credentials are auto-detected from EKS IRSA, ECS
// task role, or EC2 IMDSv2 workload identity instead (see below).
let store = S3Storage::from_env()?;
let key = store.put("avatars/42.png", &file_bytes, "image/png")?;
```

`S3Storage` signs every request with AWS Signature V4 via the outbound HTTP client (`hmac`+`sha2`) â€”
no AWS SDK. Path-style addressing (`{endpoint}/{bucket}/{key}`) works against every S3-compatible
provider. `LocalStorage` rejects `..` key segments to prevent escaping its root directory.

**Workload identity** â€” when static keys aren't set, `S3Storage` auto-detects short-lived credentials
in this order: EKS IRSA (`AWS_ROLE_ARN`+`AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE`, via STS
`AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`), ECS task role (`AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_FULL_URI`/`_RELATIVE_URI`),
then EC2 IMDSv2 (last resort, short timeout so non-EC2 hosts fail fast). `RWS_S3_CREDENTIAL_SOURCE=
static|irsa|ecs|imds` forces a source. Credentials are cached and refreshed shortly before expiry.
No XML/JSON parser dependency â€” STS/IMDS/ECS responses are read with hand-rolled field extractors
(`src/storage/aws_credentials.rs`), the same idiom already used for JWT claims elsewhere in the crate.

```rust,no_run
use rust_web_server::storage::{AzureBlobStorage, Storage}; // requires `storage-azure` feature

// Reads RWS_AZURE_ACCOUNT, RWS_AZURE_CONTAINER, and optionally RWS_AZURE_ENDPOINT
// (point at Azurite / a private endpoint). RWS_AZURE_ACCOUNT_KEY is optional â€”
// when unset, a Managed Identity OAuth token is auto-detected instead (see below).
let store = AzureBlobStorage::from_env()?;
let key = store.put("avatars/42.png", &file_bytes, "image/png")?;
```

`AzureBlobStorage` signs every request with the Shared Key HMAC-SHA256 scheme via the outbound
HTTP client (`hmac`+`sha2`) â€” no Azure SDK.

**Workload identity** â€” when the static account key isn't set, `AzureBlobStorage` auto-detects a
Managed Identity OAuth token in this order: App Service/Container Apps (`IDENTITY_ENDPOINT`+
`IDENTITY_HEADER`, injected by the platform), then VM/AKS IMDS (last resort, short timeout so
non-Azure hosts fail fast). `RWS_AZURE_CREDENTIAL_SOURCE=key|managed-identity` forces a source.
Tokens are cached and refreshed shortly before expiry. No Azure SDK or JSON parser dependency â€”
token responses use the same hand-rolled field extractor idiom as `aws_credentials`
(`src/storage/azure_credentials.rs`); unlike AWS's ISO8601 `Expiration`, Azure's `expires_on` is
already Unix epoch seconds, so no date parsing is needed.

---

## Secrets manager integration (src/secrets/mod.rs, requires `secrets` feature)

`secrets::resolve(value)` resolves a secret-*reference* string to its live value over HTTP(S); a
value that doesn't match a recognized prefix passes through unchanged â€” additive to every existing
`RWS_CONFIG_*`/`RWS_*` env var, not a breaking change.

```rust,no_run
use rust_web_server::secrets;

// VAULT_ADDR / VAULT_TOKEN must already be set in the environment.
let db_password = secrets::resolve("vault://secret/myapp/db#password")?;
# Ok::<(), rust_web_server::secrets::SecretsError>(())
```

| Prefix | Backend | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `vault://path#field` | HashiCorp Vault (KV v2) | `vault://secret/myapp/db#password` |
| `aws-sm://name` or `aws-sm://name#field` | AWS Secrets Manager | `aws-sm://prod/db-password` |
| `azkv://vault-name/secret-name` | Azure Key Vault | `azkv://my-kv/db-password` |

- **Vault** â€” `GET /v1/{mount}/data/{path}` (KV v2 HTTP API), `X-Vault-Token` header. `VAULT_ADDR`
  (default `http://127.0.0.1:8200`) and `VAULT_TOKEN` (required). Token auth only â€” no AppRole,
  Kubernetes auth, or renewal.
- **AWS Secrets Manager** â€” reuses `storage-s3`'s SigV4 signer and IRSA/ECS/IMDSv2 credential chain
  (`storage::aws_credentials::CredentialsProvider`) rather than a second copy of AWS's auth story.
  Region from `AWS_REGION`/`AWS_DEFAULT_REGION`; static keys from `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`/
  `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` if set, else the same workload-identity auto-detection `S3Storage` uses.
  `aws-sm://name#field` parses the secret's `SecretString` as JSON and extracts one field â€” the
  common "one secret holds `{"username":...,"password":...}`" shape. `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_SECRETSMANAGER`
  overrides the default `secretsmanager.{region}.amazonaws.com` host (same env var name the official
  AWS SDKs use for LocalStack/VPC-endpoint overrides).
- **Azure Key Vault** â€” `GET https://{vault-name}.vault.azure.net/secrets/{name}?api-version=7.4`,
  `Authorization: Bearer {token}`. Token comes from a service-principal client-credentials grant
  (`AZURE_KEY_VAULT_TENANT_ID`/`AZURE_KEY_VAULT_CLIENT_ID`/`AZURE_KEY_VAULT_CLIENT_SECRET`) if all
  three are set, else the same Managed Identity auto-detection `AzureBlobStorage` uses
  (`storage::azure_credentials`), requesting a token for Key Vault's resource URI instead of Storage's.

**Automatic env var resolution** â€” `secrets::resolve_env_vars()` scans every currently-set
`RWS_`-prefixed environment variable and rewrites in place (`std::env::set_var`) any whose value
matches one of the prefixes above. `Server::setup()` calls this automatically once the `secrets`
feature is enabled (right after `entry_point::bootstrap()`, so it sees the fully layered config) â€”
so any `RWS_CONFIG_*` value, or a config-driven proxy's `token_env`-referenced variable, can be a
secret reference with no code changes.

**Fail fast, not fail open** â€” a value that *looks* like a secret reference but fails to resolve
(wrong token, unreachable backend, missing field, ...) is a hard startup error (`Server::setup()`
returns `Err`, and `main()`'s `.expect("server setup failed")` panics) rather than silently ignored â€”
the alternative would be a server starting up with a JWT signing key or DB password literally equal
to the string `"vault://secret/myapp/db#password"`.

No vendor SDK for any of the three backends â€” all HTTP calls go through the existing
`http_client::Client`, and JSON responses are parsed via `service_discovery::json_lite` (shared with
`service_discovery`'s Consul/etcd backends rather than a third hand-rolled JSON parser).

---

## OpenAPI / Swagger (src/openapi/mod.rs, requires `openapi` feature)

Generates an OpenAPI 3.0 spec directly from registered routes â€” no separate spec file to maintain.

```rust
use rust_web_server::app::App;
use rust_web_server::openapi::OpenApiConfig;
use rust_web_server::response::Response;
use rust_web_server::core::New;

let app = App::with_state(db)
    .get("/users", list_users)
    .get("/users/:id", get_user)
    .openapi(OpenApiConfig::new("My API", "1.0.0")); // call last â€” snapshots routes registered so far
```

Adds `GET /openapi.json` (the generated spec, `application/json`) and `GET /docs` (Swagger UI via the
`unpkg.com/swagger-ui-dist` CDN, pointed at `/openapi.json`). `AsyncAppWithState::openapi(config)` works
the same way for `async fn` handlers (requires `http2`).

Scope: paths, methods, and path parameters only (`:id`/`*path` â†’ `{id}`/`{path}` with a `parameters`
entry). No request/response body schemas â€” Rust has no runtime type reflection to extract a JSON
Schema from a `#[derive(Validate)]` struct, so every operation gets a generic `200 OK` response.

---

## Per-route timeouts (src/timeout/mod.rs)

A single global read timeout applies to every route by default. Wrap individual handlers to give
them their own budget â€” a file upload might need 120s, a health check should fail at 500ms.

```rust
use rust_web_server::router::Router;
use rust_web_server::timeout::with_timeout;
use std::time::Duration;

let router = Router::new()
    .get("/healthz", with_timeout(Duration::from_millis(500), health_handler))
    .post("/upload", with_timeout(Duration::from_secs(120), upload_handler));
```

`with_timeout_state` is the `AppWithState<S>` equivalent (requires `S: Clone`). `with_timeout_async`
is the `AsyncAppWithState<S>` equivalent (requires `http2`, no `Clone` bound, genuine cancellation via
`tokio::time::timeout`). `TimeoutLayer::new`/`::from_arc` wraps a whole `Application`.

Config-driven proxy: `timeout_ms` as a flat key under `[route.middleware]`:
```toml
[route.middleware]
timeout_ms = 120000
```

**Honest limitation**: Rust cannot forcibly stop a running synchronous thread. The sync variants
(`with_timeout`, `with_timeout_state`, `TimeoutLayer`) run the wrapped work on a background thread and
return `504 Gateway Timeout` as soon as the deadline passes, but a handler that ignores its deadline
keeps running to completion in the background â€” this bounds the client's wait time, not the handler's
resource usage. Only `with_timeout_async` gets genuine cancellation (dropping a suspended `Future`
actually stops it).

## Per-route max body size (config-driven proxy)

```toml
[route.middleware]
max_body_size = 65536   # 413 if this route's request body exceeds 64 KiB
```

`0`/absent means no route-specific limit (same convention as `timeout_ms`). This is a *stricter,
additional* cap on top of the global `RWS_CONFIG_MAX_BODY_SIZE_IN_BYTES` â€” not a substitute for it.
It's checked by `PerRouteMaxBodySize` after `RouteMatcher` has already resolved the route, which
means the body has necessarily already been fully read (route matching requires the complete
`Request`); it protects this route's own logic/upstream from a body that's within the global limit
but too big for this specific route, not server memory from an unbounded upload â€” that's what the
global env var is for.

---

## Request ID / correlation ID middleware (src/request_id/mod.rs)

```rust
use rust_web_server::app::App;
use rust_web_server::core::New;
use rust_web_server::request_id::RequestIdLayer;

let app = App::new().wrap(RequestIdLayer::new());
```

Ensures every request/response pair carries a stable `X-Request-Id` value:
- Incoming request already has the header (set by an upstream gateway/service)? Echoed back unchanged
  â€” the same ID follows the request across service boundaries.
- Otherwise, generates one (`generate_request_id()` â€” UUID-v4-shaped, not crypto-random, fine for log
  correlation) and injects it into the request *before* the handler runs, so handlers can read it like
  any other header.
- Always set on the response too.

`.header("X-Correlation-Id")` overrides the header name. Read it in a handler via the `RequestId`
extractor (`rust_web_server::extract::RequestId`, never fails â€” empty string if unset) or any normal
header-reading path. Complements `OtelLayer`'s W3C `traceparent` spans rather than replacing them â€”
`X-Request-Id` is a simple app-level ID; `traceparent` is the OTEL distributed-tracing context.

---

## Directory listing (src/app/controller/static_resource/mod.rs, src/app/controller/directory_listing/mod.rs)

Default, always-on behavior â€” not a feature flag. Any requested directory with no `index.html` now
renders a directory listing page (200 OK) instead of `404 Not Found`; a directory that *does* have an
`index.html` is served exactly as before.

```rust
use rust_web_server::app::App;
use rust_web_server::test_client::TestClient;

let client = TestClient::new(App::new());
let response = client.get("/static/no_index/").send(); // no index.html in this dir
assert_eq!(200, response.status());
```

`StaticResourceController::render_directory_listing(fs_dir_path, request_path)` builds the page: a
breadcrumb, a parent-directory link (omitted at a static root), and a table (directories first, then
files, both sorted case-insensitively) with size/modified columns; a client-side search box filters
rows. Entry names are HTML-escaped for display and percent-encoded (`URL::percent_encode`) in `href`s.
Dotfiles are omitted.

The page's CSS/JS are same-origin assets at `/rws-directory-listing.css` / `/rws-directory-listing.js`
(`DirectoryListingAssetsController`), **not** inlined `<style>`/`<script>` â€” the framework's default
`Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'` would silently block inline blocks. Like
`StyleController`/`ScriptController`, a file on disk at that same relative path overrides the
compiled-in default (`include_bytes!`) without recompiling.

---

## SPA fallback (src/app/controller/static_resource/mod.rs)

Opt-in (unset by default). `RWS_CONFIG_SPA_FALLBACK=index.html` serves that file for a `GET`/`HEAD`
request matching no real file/directory/`path.html`, instead of `404` â€” the client-side-router ("SPA")
fallback React Router/Vue Router/etc. deep links need.

```bash
cd dist && RWS_CONFIG_SPA_FALLBACK=index.html rws
```

```rust
use rust_web_server::app::App;
use rust_web_server::test_client::TestClient;

let client = TestClient::new(App::new());
let response = client.get("/dashboard/settings").send(); // no file on disk
assert_eq!(200, response.status()); // served index.html instead of 404
```

Scoped by two checks, both automatic: (1) only a request whose *last path segment* has no `.` gets the
fallback â€” `/dashboard/settings` qualifies, `/assets/logo.png` (a missed static asset) still 404s, the
same heuristic webpack-dev-server's `historyApiFallback`/`sirv`/`vite preview` use; (2)
`RWS_CONFIG_SPA_FALLBACK_EXCLUDE_PREFIXES` (comma-separated, e.g. `/api,/healthz`) never gets the
fallback even when configured, so a real API 404 isn't silently rewritten into a `200`. The fallback
file must actually exist on disk â€” a typo'd config value is a silent no-op, not a broken response. Read
fresh every request (`entry_point::get_spa_fallback`/`get_spa_fallback_exclude_prefixes`), so it takes
effect via `rws.config.toml` + `SIGHUP` with no restart.

---

## HTML templates (requires `tera` feature)

```rust
use rust_web_server::template;

template::init("templates/");  // call once at startup

// In handler:
let mut ctx = tera::Context::new();
ctx.insert("name", "Alice");
let response = template::render("index.html", &ctx);
```

Hot reload without restarting: `template::reload()` re-globs the template directory (edited content, added/removed files); on a syntax error it returns `Err` and keeps the previous templates serving rather than a half-broken set. Wired automatically into `config_reload::reload()`'s `SIGHUP` hook â€” a no-op if `template::init()` was never called.

---

## Virtual hosting / SNI routing

```rust
use rust_web_server::virtual_host::VirtualHostConfig;
use rust_web_server::router::Router;

// In Server::run_tls, pass multiple VirtualHostConfig entries:
let vhosts = vec![
    VirtualHostConfig { domain: "api.example.com".into(),  cert_file: "api.pem".into(),  key_file: "api.key".into() },
    VirtualHostConfig { domain: "www.example.com".into(),  cert_file: "www.pem".into(),  key_file: "www.key".into() },
];

// Per-host routing via Router:
let api_router = Router::new().with_host("api.example.com").get("/status", status_handler);
let www_router = Router::new().with_host("www.example.com").get("/", home_handler);
```

---

## Graceful shutdown

- **http1**: `SIGINT`/`SIGTERM` â†’ `AtomicBool` stops accept loop; `ThreadPool` drains in-flight requests.
- **http2/http3**: `tokio::signal` handlers call `select!` on the accept loop; `SERVER_READY` is cleared before exit.
- **`/readyz`** returns 503 while draining. **`/healthz`** always returns 200.

---

## Security checklist

- PKCE (RFC 7636) â€” `sso` module; `PkceVerifier::new()` + `verifier.challenge()`
- CSRF double-submit cookie â€” `csrf` module; constant-time comparison
- Rate limiting â€” `RateLimitLayer` or `rate_limit::global().check(ip)`
- Distributed rate limiting â€” `RedisRateLimiter` (fixed-window, backed by Redis via hand-rolled RESP client); shares a budget across multiple `rws` instances, unlike the in-process `RateLimiter`
- Max request body size â€” `RWS_CONFIG_MAX_BODY_SIZE_IN_BYTES`; 413 before buffering, across HTTP/1.1/2/3
- `Expect: 100-continue` (HTTP/1.1 only) â€” server sends `100 Continue` before reading the body; a size-limit 413 or an unsupported `Expect` value's 417 is sent instead, without reading the body at all
- mTLS â€” set `RWS_CONFIG_TLS_CLIENT_CA_FILE`
- IP filtering â€” `IpFilter::allow([â€¦])` / `.deny([â€¦])`
- JWT HS256 â€” `auth` module; constant-time HMAC
- JWT RS256/ES256 without a JWKS endpoint â€” `JwtLayer::rs256`/`::es256`; static PEM public key, `auth-asymmetric` feature
- Forward-auth to an external service â€” `auth::forward::ForwardAuthLayer`; fails closed (502) if the auth service is unreachable
- JWT RS256/ES256 â€” `sso::JwksCache`; signature + expiry + aud + iss validated
- Webhook signature verification â€” `webhook::verify_webhook_signature`; GitHub/Shopify/Stripe HMAC-SHA256, constant-time compare, Stripe replay-window timestamp check
- Argon2id password hashing â€” `crypto` module; random salt; PHC string output
- Signed/encrypted cookies â€” `cookie::signed_cookie` (HMAC-SHA256) / `cookie::encrypted_cookie` (AES-256-GCM); fails closed (`None`) on tampering or wrong key
- `SameSite=Strict` session cookies â€” set via `session_cookie()` helper
- `HttpOnly` flag â€” controllable on all `Set-Cookie` builders
- XSS: no built-in output escaping â€” use Tera templates which auto-escape HTML
- Directory listing â€” entry names HTML-escaped for display, percent-encoded in `href`s; dotfiles omitted; CSS/JS served as same-origin assets rather than inline blocks so the default CSP needs no relaxation
- SPA fallback â€” opt-in only (`RWS_CONFIG_SPA_FALLBACK`, unset by default); scoped by a no-extension-in-last-segment heuristic plus `RWS_CONFIG_SPA_FALLBACK_EXCLUDE_PREFIXES` so a real API `404` isn't silently rewritten into a `200`
- Secrets-manager references â€” `secrets::resolve` (Vault/AWS Secrets Manager/Azure Key Vault, `secrets` feature); a broken `vault://`/`aws-sm://`/`azkv://` reference is a hard startup error, never a silent fallback to the literal reference string

---

## Module index

```
src/app/                â€” App (zero-config) and controller dispatch
src/application/        â€” Application trait
src/async_state/        â€” AsyncAppWithState
src/auth/               â€” BasicAuthLayer, JwtLayer, build_jwt, verify_jwt (auth feature)
                          JwtLayer::rs256/::es256, verify_jwt_rs256/verify_jwt_es256 (auth-asymmetric feature)
src/auth/forward.rs     â€” ForwardAuthLayer, delegates auth decisions to an external HTTP service (auth feature)
src/blocklist/          â€” IP blocklist middleware
src/body/               â€” multipart_form_data, form_urlencoded parsers
src/cache/              â€” CacheLayer middleware
src/canary/             â€” CanaryLayer smooth-weighted-round-robin traffic splitting (plain HTTP or
                          TLS backends); WeightedPool for BackendPool-sourced groups; live .update()
src/circuit_breaker/    â€” CircuitBreaker, RedisCircuitBreaker (persistent), RetryLayer, Breaker trait
                          (ReverseProxy auto-wiring), max_half_open_probes cap, all_states() (metrics)
src/client_hint/        â€” Client Hints header parsing
src/compression/        â€” gzip compression applied in Server::process
src/config_reload/      â€” hot-reload logic; ConfigSnapshot
src/controller/         â€” Controller trait; built-in controllers
src/cookie/             â€” CookieJar, SetCookie builder
src/cookie/crypto_ext.rs â€” signed_cookie, verify_signed_cookie, encrypted_cookie, decrypt_cookie (crypto feature)
src/core/               â€” New trait (used for App::new())
src/cors/               â€” CORS handler controller
src/csrf/               â€” CsrfLayer, CsrfToken (csrf feature)
src/crypto/             â€” hash_password, verify_password, generate_token (crypto feature)
src/di/                 â€” Container (dependency injection)
src/entry_point/        â€” Config constants, CLI arg parsing, startup
src/error/              â€” IntoResponse trait, AppError enum
src/ext/                â€” Extension traits
src/extract/            â€” FromRequest trait, Body, BodyText, Query, RequestHeaders
src/feature/            â€” Feature flag introspection
src/h2_handler/         â€” HTTP/2 connection handler (http2 feature)
src/h3_handler/         â€” HTTP/3 connection handler (http3 feature)
src/header/             â€” Header struct; header name constants
src/http/               â€” HTTP version constants
src/http_client/        â€” Client, AsyncClient, outbound HTTP
src/ingress/            â€” KubernetesIngressWatcher, IngressRouter
src/ip_filter/          â€” IpFilter middleware
src/jobs/               â€” Job, JobQueue, PersistentJobQueue (jobs feature)
src/json/               â€” Json<T> extractor/responder (serde feature)
src/language/           â€” Accept-Language parsing
src/log/                â€” Combined Log Format + JSON log helpers
src/macros/             â€” routes! macro and utility macros
src/maintenance/        â€” MaintenanceLayer
src/mcp/                â€” McpServer (MCP Streamable HTTP), McpContext (per-request tool context), ToolAnnotations (2025-03-26 tool hints)
src/metrics/            â€” Prometheus metrics, MetricsLayer
src/middleware/         â€” Middleware trait, WithMiddleware, RateLimitLayer
src/mime_type/          â€” MIME type constants and detection
src/model/              â€” Async ORM (sqlx): DbPool, DbTransaction, Backend, Model, Repository, QueryBuilder
src/null/               â€” NullApplication (returns 404 for everything)
src/openapi/            â€” OpenApiConfig, build_spec, swagger_ui_html (openapi feature)
src/otel/               â€” OtelLayer, Span (nested child spans via span()/client_span()), setup, span export
src/pagination/         â€” Page<T>, CursorPage<T>, RFC 8288 Link header builder (no feature gate)
src/prelude/            â€” Re-exports of the most commonly used types
src/proxy/              â€” ReverseProxy, H2ReverseProxy, GrpcProxy, ConnPool (pool.rs)
src/async_bridge/       â€” block_on_isolated: sync/async bridge for Middleware/Application
                          impls that need to call async code (http2 feature)
src/proxy_config/       â€” Config-driven proxy: ProxyConfig, ConfigDrivenApp, StaticAdapter
src/range/              â€” Range, ContentRange, body construction
src/rate_limit/         â€” RateLimiter, global(), RedisRateLimiter (distributed, Redis-backed)
src/redis_protocol/     â€” RespConn: crate-internal hand-rolled RESP v2 client shared by
                          RedisSessionStore and RedisRateLimiter (not public API)
src/request/            â€” Request struct and HTTP/1.1 parsing
src/request_id/         â€” RequestIdLayer, generate_request_id
src/request_log/        â€” Per-request log middleware
src/response/           â€” Response struct, STATUS_CODE_REASON_PHRASE,
                          Response::json/Response::text one-line helpers (no serde needed)
src/rewrite/            â€” RewriteLayer (request/response rewriting)
                          request_uri_regex_rewrite (regex URI rewrite) (rewrite-regex feature)
src/router/             â€” Router, PathParams, named/wildcard routing
src/scheduler/          â€” Scheduler (fixed-rate, fixed-delay, cron)
src/server/             â€” Server (bind, accept, process), ConnectionInfo, Address
src/service_discovery/  â€” BackendPool, DiscoverySource (Static/EnvPrefix/File/Dns/DnsSrv/Consul/
                          Docker/EtcdWatch); json_lite (crate-internal JSON parser used by
                          Consul/Docker/etcd); dns_srv (hand-rolled RFC 1035/2782 SRV codec)
src/secrets/            â€” secrets::resolve, SecretsError, resolve_env_vars (secrets feature);
                          vault (HashiCorp Vault KV v2), aws_secrets_manager (reuses storage's
                          SigV4 signer + credential chain), azure_key_vault (service principal +
                          reused Managed Identity token fetch)
src/session/            â€” SessionStore, Session, cookie helpers
src/mailer/             â€” Mailer, Email, EmailBuilder, SmtpTls, MailerError (mailer feature)
src/sso/                â€” OidcAuth, OidcConfig, OidcProvider, JwksCache (sso feature);
                          AuthServer, ClientStore, OAuthClient (sso-server feature);
                          saml::{SamlSp, SamlConfig, SamlIdpMetadata, AttributeMap} (sso-saml feature)
src/state/              â€” AppWithState<S>
src/storage/            â€” Storage, LocalStorage, S3Storage, AzureBlobStorage (storage-local /
                          storage-s3 / storage-azure features); aws_credentials â€” EKS IRSA / ECS /
                          EC2 IMDSv2 workload-identity credentials; azure_credentials â€” Managed
                          Identity credentials (App Service/Container Apps / VM/AKS IMDS)
src/symbol/             â€” ASCII/byte constants
src/tcp_proxy/          â€” TcpProxy (L4)
src/template/           â€” TeraEngine, global init/render/reload (tera feature)
src/test_client/        â€” TestClient
src/thread_pool/        â€” ThreadPool (http1 feature)
src/timeout/            â€” with_timeout, with_timeout_state, with_timeout_async, TimeoutLayer
src/tls/                â€” SniCertResolver, TlsAcceptor builders (http2 feature)
src/udp_proxy/          â€” UdpProxy
src/url/                â€” URL parsing, percent-encode/decode
src/validate/           â€” Validate trait, Validated<T>, ValidationErrors
src/virtual_host/       â€” VirtualHostConfig
src/webhook/            â€” verify_webhook_signature, WebhookProvider (GitHub/Shopify/Stripe) (webhook feature)
src/websocket/          â€” WebSocket handshake, Frame codec
src/ws_proxy/           â€” WsProxy (WebSocket proxy); with_live_backends() for health-check-aware routing
```

---

## Cargo.toml snippet for common setups

```toml
# Full stack â€” HTTP/3 + JSON + auth + sessions + crypto + CSRF + SSO + ORM + templates
[dependencies]
rust-web-server = { version = "17", features = [
    "serde", "auth", "macros", "tera",
    "model-sqlite", "crypto", "csrf", "sso"
] }

# API server â€” HTTP/3 + JSON + auth
[dependencies]
rust-web-server = { version = "17", features = ["serde", "auth", "macros"] }

# HTTP/1.1 only (smallest binary, sync, no TLS)
[dependencies]
rust-web-server = { version = "17", default-features = false, features = ["http1"] }
```
